Professor View 01 / 27
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Emmanuele Orrico

Business English para as reuniões com o time Global da Sanofi, congressos médicos e a apresentação de agosto

A2 (Básico) Gerente Nacional de Demanda — Sanofi Imunologia 60 min · Online · 2x/semana
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Nome

Emmanuele Orrico

Idade

44 anos

Empresa

Sanofi — indústria farmacêutica (matriz francesa)

Profissão

Gerente Nacional de Demanda — Imunologia (8 doenças: dermatite atópica, asma, DPOC, rinossinusite...)

Nível

A2 (Básico) — compreende bem; a produção oral está próxima de A1 e trava sob pressão

Foco

100% Business — reuniões com a Global, apresentação de resultados, congressos médicos, jantares com executivos. (Inglês de sobrevivência/viagem fica nas aulas em GRUPO.)

Frequência

2x por semana (segundas e sextas, 17h) — Online (Zoom), 60 min

Total de Aulas

33 aulas (60 min)

Personagem-Jornada

De: Executiva que domina imunologia, acesso a mercado e execução de campo — e trava por completo quando a conversa vira para o inglês. Ela mesma definiu: “Se você tentar falar comigo em inglês aqui agora eu vou travar. Quando você desligar, eu vou: ai, ela falou isso.” Ciclos repetidos de tentativa e abandono: “não vai ser to be esse inferno na minha vida, que eu já aprendi, desaprendi, aprendi, desaprendi.”

Para: Gerente que, na última semana de agosto, apresenta resultados, desafios e oportunidades do Brasil para o time Global da Sanofi — em inglês, de pé, sem pedir para outra pessoa falar por ela. Ela disse: “Eu deveria fazer, se eu tiver coragem.” O programa inteiro existe para transformar esse “se” em “vou”.

Promessa Transformadora

O problema da Emmanuele não é conhecimento — ela descreve com precisão cirúrgica perfil de paciente, PCDT, SUS, operadoras e dinâmica competitiva contra Lilly, AstraZeneca e GSK. O problema é o canal de saída: o raciocínio dela tenta traduzir o tempo inteiro e a frase não se forma a tempo. Cada aula ataca isso com o material real dela — resultados, lançamentos, brand plan, time de campo, reunião com a Global — e entrega andaimes (frases prontas, sentence starters) para que ela nunca fique em silêncio. A Alumni é “a minha última cartada”: o material não pode parecer mais um curso.

Forças
  • Domínio profundo da área — o problema é o código (inglês), não a mensagem
  • Autoconsciência rara sobre o próprio bloqueio: “meu raciocínio ainda demora demais para conectar, tenta traduzir o tempo inteiro”
  • Contexto de uso altíssimo e definido: Global, resultados, Rain Plan, congressos, jantares
  • Hábito auditivo diário já instalado: “todo dia eu consigo ouvir podcast”
  • Evento-alvo com data: última semana de agosto de 2026, apresentação ao time Global
  • Aprende praticando: “eu funciono muito praticando”
Pontos de Melhoria
  • Produção oral praticamente inexistente em contexto real — travamento total sob pressão
  • Tradução mental constante impede fluidez conversacional
  • Histórico de inconsistência e baixa priorização — risco real de abandono
  • Pre-class ainda percebido como obrigação: “vai ter que fazer, eu vou ter que me esforçar”
  • Participação passiva nas reuniões — não assume protagonismo em inglês
  • Baixa tolerância ao erro — exige segurança psicológica desde a aula 1

Currículo Completo — 33 Aulas

Metodologia

1. A gramática NUNCA é o título da aula

Ela foi explícita: “não vai ser to be esse inferno na minha vida.” Nenhuma aula se chama “verbo X”. A estrutura aparece DENTRO do contexto dela (resultados, lançamentos, reunião com a Global) e sempre por discovery: exemplos primeiro, regra depois. Zero conjugação descontextualizada.

2. Andaimes contra o travamento

Ela trava e traduz mentalmente. Toda aula entrega frases prontas (sentence starters, survival card) que ela só precisa PEGAR, não construir. Produção livre sem apoio, no começo, só reforça o bloqueio.

3. 100% Business (o particular)

Combinado com a consultora: viagem e inglês de sobrevivência ficam nas aulas em grupo. Aqui: imunologia, dermatite atópica, asma, DPOC, acesso, brand plan, congresso, jantar com executivo. Nada de aeroporto, hotel ou restaurante.

4. Praticar é o método

“Eu funciono muito praticando.” Estilo cinestésico-auditivo: role-play, simulação, escuta ativa com resposta oral imediata. Atividade nova a cada 12-15 minutos — nunca 20 minutos de explicação.

5. Progresso visível e erro normalizado

Ela já desistiu de vários cursos por “nunca conseguir avançar”. O professor nomeia em voz alta o que ela conseguiu fazer em cada aula. Baseline gravado na aula 1, recomparado na 17. Tolerância ao erro baixa — segurança psicológica desde o primeiro minuto.

Mapa de Personalidade

IntrovertidoExtrovertido
LúdicoSério, orientado a resultado
ImpulsivoReflexivo
Aprendiz leitura/escritaAprendiz cinestésico (praticando)
Tolerância ao erro baixaTolerância ao erro alta
Insegura em inglêsConfiante em inglês
Foco acadêmicoFoco profissional
Lesson 01 -- Pre-class

Diagnostic + First Words -- Who Is Emmanuele Orrico?

Introduce yourself and describe your job on a call with the Sanofi Global team. Key words: pharmaceutical company, immunology, national manager, field team, global meeting, results, launch, disease area, to be responsible for, to manage. Structures: I work at... / I manage... / I am responsible for... (facts about your job), the -s in my team visits, and the ready phrases to ask people to repeat or to speak more slowly.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words you use at work every day.

Pharmaceutical company -- a company that makes medicines
"Sanofi is a pharmaceutical company."
Immunology -- the study of how the body fights disease
"I work in immunology."
National manager -- the person who leads a business in the whole country
"I am the national demand manager for Brazil."
Field team -- the people who visit doctors in hospitals and clinics
"My field team visits doctors every week."
Global meeting -- a meeting with colleagues from other countries
"We have a global meeting every month."
Results -- the numbers that show how the business is going
"I present the results to the Global team."
Launch -- when a company puts a new medicine on the market
"The launch starts in September."
Disease area -- a group of illnesses that one team takes care of
"Asthma is one of my disease areas."
To be responsible for -- to take care of something as part of your job
"I am responsible for eight disease areas."
To manage -- to lead a team or a business
"I manage the immunology portfolio in Brazil."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Pharmaceutical company
Immunology
National manager
Field team
Global meeting
Results
Launch
Disease area
To be responsible for
To manage

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Emmanuele Orrico works at Sanofi, a pharmaceutical company. She is the national demand manager in Brazil, and she manages the immunology portfolio. "I am responsible for eight disease areas," she says. "Asthma, COPD and atopic dermatitis, for example." Her field team visits doctors every week, and the team talks about the patients and the treatments. Every month, Emmanuele presents the results in a global meeting with colleagues from France, Canada and Japan. She does not work in oncology, and she does not manage the factory. She manages the demand. In September, the company starts a new launch, and in August the Global team visits Brazil. "I present the results, the challenges and the opportunities," she says.

1. Why do we say "My field team visits doctors" and not "My field team visit doctors"?
A Because "my team" is ONE group (like he / she / it) — so the verb takes -s.
B Because the action is happening right now, at this moment.
C Because the action happened in the past.
2. "I am responsible for eight disease areas." In this sentence, "am responsible for" is:
A An action she is doing right now.
B A READY PHRASE to say what you take care of at work — use the whole block, do not build it.
C A way to talk about the future.
3. "She does not work in oncology." What does this sentence say?
A She worked in oncology in the past.
B Oncology is NOT part of her job — the negative uses does not (doesn't) + verb.
C She is going to start working in oncology.
4. Which sentence about her job is correct?
A "She work in Sanofi and responsible for eight disease areas."
B "She works at Sanofi and she is responsible for eight disease areas."
C "She works in Sanofi and she responsible for eight disease areas."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Talking About Your Job: I work at / I manage / I am responsible for

GRAMMAR

The code that opens every call with the Global team: how to say who you are and what you take care of.

StructureUseExample
I / you / we / they + verbFacts about your job.I work at Sanofi.
he / she / my team + verb + sThe SAME idea, but for one person or ONE group. This -s is easy to forget.My team visits doctors.
READY PHRASE: I am responsible for + thingThere is nothing to build. Take the whole block and use it.I am responsible for eight disease areas.
Negative: don't / doesn't + verbTo say what is NOT your job.I don't work in oncology.
Question: Do / Does + person + verb?To ASK your colleague something (not only to answer).Do you work in immunology?
at vs inA company takes at: "I work at Sanofi". An area or a country takes in: "I work in immunology", "I work in Brazil".

Careful: "responsible" is not a verb, so "I responsible for..." does not exist in English. Always say the full block: I am responsible for... — one piece, no thinking. And a company always takes at: "I work at Sanofi", never "in Sanofi".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I at Sanofi, in Brazil."
"I eight disease areas."
"My field team doctors every week."
"I the immunology portfolio in Brazil."
"I in oncology."
"I work Sanofi."

Stage 2: Put the Global Call in Order

Order

Put the steps of a first call with the Global team in the correct order.

?Talk about your field team and the disease areas you take care of.
?Introduce yourself: your name and the company you work at.
?Describe your role: what you manage and what you are responsible for.
?Confirm that you present the results to the Global team in August.
?Thank the Global team for the call and confirm everyone can hear you.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences that open any meeting with the Global team.

My name is Emmanuele Orrico, and I work at Sanofi.
I am the national demand manager for immunology.
I am responsible for eight disease areas.
My field team visits doctors every week.
Sorry, could you repeat that, please?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each situation.

Marc, from the Global team, asks: "So, what do you do at Sanofi?" You answer:
A "I do the immunology things in Brazil."
B "I am the national demand manager, and I manage the immunology portfolio in Brazil."
C "I responsible for the immunology in Sanofi."
He asks: "Which disease areas are you responsible for?" The best answer is:
A "I responsible for eight disease areas."
B "I am responsible for eight disease areas: asthma, COPD and atopic dermatitis, for example."
C "Eight disease areas is my responsible."
You want to talk about your field team. Which sentence is correct?
A "My field team visits doctors every week."
B "My field team visit doctors every week."
C "My field team is visit doctors every week."
Someone asks if you take care of oncology. You do NOT. You say:
A "I no work in oncology."
B "I not work in oncology."
C "I don't work in oncology. I work in immunology."
The question was too fast and you did NOT understand. The most professional move is:
A Stay quiet and hope that someone changes the subject.
B "Sorry, could you repeat that, please?" — or "Could you speak more slowly, please?"
C Say sorry for your English and stop talking in the meeting.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 60 seconds, with no script. This recording is your BASELINE: we will listen to it again in lesson 17.

You are starting a video call with the Sanofi Global team. Introduce yourself: your name and your company (I work at...), your role (I am the national demand manager), what you take care of (I am responsible for...), and what your field team does every week (My team visits...). Finish with one sentence about August, when the Global team visits Brazil. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1My name is Emmanuele Orrico, and I work at Sanofi.
2I am the national demand manager for immunology.
3I am responsible for eight disease areas.
4Sorry, could you repeat that, please?
5Let me think for a second.
Lesson 02 -- Pre-class

Your Day and Your Work Routine -- A Week at Sanofi Brazil

Describe your typical week to the Global team: Monday with the demand plan, the field visit, the global call on Thursday, the reports on Friday. Key words: schedule, report, field visit, medical rep, demand plan, headquarters, to attend, to review, to prepare, to travel. Structures: present simple for your routine + always / usually / often / sometimes / never (always BEFORE the verb), the negative I don't travel and the question Do you...? Expression of the lesson: "It is part of the job."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words that are in your week, every week.

Schedule -- the plan of your week: what happens and when
"My schedule is full on Mondays."
Report -- a document with numbers and information about the business
"I read the field reports every Tuesday."
Field visit -- a day outside the office, with the team, visiting doctors
"I do a field visit twice a month."
Medical rep -- the person who talks to doctors about our medicines
"Every medical rep visits ten doctors a week."
Demand plan -- the plan that says how much medicine the market needs
"We review the demand plan every Monday."
Headquarters -- the main office of the company
"Our headquarters are in Paris."
To attend -- to go to a meeting or a call
"I attend the global call every Thursday."
To review -- to look at something again and check it
"I review the numbers before the meeting."
To prepare -- to get something ready before it happens
"I prepare the presentation on Fridays."
To travel -- to go to another city for work
"I sometimes travel to other cities."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Schedule
Report
Field visit
Medical rep
Demand plan
Headquarters
To attend
To review
To prepare
To travel

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Emmanuele always starts her week with numbers. On Monday, she reviews the demand plan with her team and she checks the schedule for the week. On Tuesday, the medical reps usually send their reports. On Wednesday, she often does a field visit and visits doctors with a rep. On Thursday, she always attends the global call with headquarters in Paris. On Friday, she prepares the reports for the next week. She sometimes travels to other cities, but she never travels on Fridays. She does not schedule meetings on Friday, and she doesn't work at the weekend. "Monday is never quiet," she says. "It is part of the job."

1. Why do we say "I always review" and not "I review always"?
A Because always, usually, often, sometimes and never come BEFORE the main verb.
B Because the frequency word always comes at the end of the sentence.
C Because the action is happening now, at this moment.
2. "The medical reps usually send their reports." If the sentence is about ONE rep, what changes?
A Nothing changes: "One rep usually send their reports."
B The verb takes -s: "One rep usually sends his reports." (the same rule as my team visits, from lesson 1)
C The verb goes to the past.
3. "She never travels on Fridays." What does this sentence say?
A That she travels on some Fridays.
B Zero Fridays: never = 0%. And note that the sentence does NOT take don't — never is already the negative.
C That she traveled last Friday.
4. Which sentence about her week is correct?
A "She attend always the global call on Thursday."
B "She always attends the global call on Thursday."
C "She always attend the global call on Thursday."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Talking About Your Week: always, usually, often, sometimes, never

GRAMMAR

How to say WHAT you do and HOW OFTEN you do it.

StructureUseExample
I / we / they + always, usually, often, sometimes, never + verbThe frequency word comes BEFORE the verb, never after it.I always review the demand plan.
he / she / my team + verb + sOne person or one team: the verb takes -s (the same rule as lesson 1).My team usually sends the reports.
Negative: I don't / she doesn't + verbTo say what is NOT in your week. Careful: with never you do not use don't (never is already the negative).I don't travel on Fridays.
Question: Do / Does + person + verb?To ASK your colleague about his week (and not only to answer).Do you travel every month?
How often? / When?always (100%) · usually · often · sometimes · never (0%). Time expressions go at the start or at the end: every week, on Mondays, twice a month.

Common mistake: many Brazilian students say "I go always to the call", with the frequency word AFTER the verb. In English this sounds wrong. The place is always BEFORE the verb: I always go. And a question needs Do at the start: "Do you attend the call?", never only "You attend the call?".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"On Monday, I the demand plan."
"The medical reps their reports on Tuesday."
"I the global call every Thursday."
"I on Fridays."
"On Friday, I the reports for the next week."
" the global call every week?"

Stage 2: Put the Week in Order

Order

Put the days of a typical week at Sanofi in the correct order.

?Friday: prepare the reports for the next week.
?Wednesday: do a field visit and see doctors with a rep.
?Thursday: attend the global call with headquarters in Paris.
?Monday: review the demand plan with the team.
?Tuesday: read the field reports from the medical reps.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These five sentences answer the question: what does your week look like?

On Monday, I always review the demand plan with my team.
I often do a field visit with a medical rep.
I attend the global call every Thursday.
I sometimes travel, but I never travel on Fridays.
It is part of the job.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each situation in a real meeting with the Global team.

Marc asks: "What do you do on Mondays?" You answer:
A "I review always the demand plan."
B "On Monday, I always review the demand plan with my team."
C "Monday I am review the demand plan."
He asks: "How often do you travel?" The best answer is:
A "I travel sometimes, but Friday no."
B "I sometimes travel to other cities, but I never travel on Fridays."
C "I don't never travel on Fridays."
You want to talk about what your team does every week. Which sentence is correct?
A "My team usually sends the field reports on Tuesday."
B "My team send usually the field reports on Tuesday."
C "My team is send the field reports on Tuesday."
You want to ASK Marc if he goes to the Monday call. You say:
A "You attend the Monday call?"
B "Attend you the Monday call?"
C "Do you attend the Monday call?"
It was a busy week and someone from Global says that. The professional and natural answer is:
A "Yes, my work is very bad this week."
B "Yes, it was a busy week. It is part of the job."
C "Yes, I am part of the job."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for 90 seconds, with no script.

Marc Dubois asks you to describe your typical week at Sanofi. Start with "Every week, I..." and talk about five days: what you always do, what you usually do, what you often do, what you sometimes do, and what you never do. Use the words from this lesson (demand plan, field visit, medical rep, reports, global call, headquarters). Finish with the expression of the lesson: "It is part of the job." Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1On Monday, I always review the demand plan with my team.
2I often do a field visit with a medical rep.
3I attend the global call every Thursday.
4I sometimes travel, but I never travel on Fridays.
5It is part of the job.
Lesson 03 -- Pre-class

What Happened Last Quarter? -- Reporting Past Results

Tell the Global team what happened last quarter: the target you achieved in dermatology, the target you missed in asthma, the market access challenge, and the growth in the private market. Key words: quarter, target, challenge, opportunity, growth, to achieve, to exceed, to miss, market access, prescription volume. Structures: past simple — regular verbs with -ed (achieved, missed, presented) and the five irregular verbs of the business review (went, had, gave, met, came), the negative didn't achieve, and the time expressions last quarter / in April / two months ago. Phrase of the lesson: "Once in a while, we have unexpected results."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words you see in every business review.

Quarter -- a period of three months in the business year
"Last quarter was very busy."
Target -- the number the company wants you to reach
"We had a high target in dermatology."
Challenge -- something difficult in the business
"We faced a challenge with market access."
Opportunity -- a chance to grow the business
"The private market is a big opportunity."
Growth -- when the numbers go up
"The growth came from the private market."
To achieve -- to reach the number you needed
"We achieved our target in dermatology."
To exceed -- to go above the number you needed
"In Q2, the team exceeded the target."
To miss -- to not reach the number you needed
"We missed the target in asthma."
Market access -- when a medicine is approved and paid for, so patients can get it
"Market access was slow in the public market."
Prescription volume -- how many prescriptions the doctors write for your medicine
"Prescription volume went up in June."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Quarter
Target
Challenge
Opportunity
Growth
To achieve
To exceed
To miss
Market access
Prescription volume

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Last quarter was busy, but Emmanuele had good news for Paris. In April, she presented the plan to the Global team. In May, her team met sixty new doctors in the private market. In June, prescription volume went up, and the growth came from the private market. In dermatology, the team exceeded the target: they achieved one hundred and eight percent. In asthma, they missed the target. The challenge was market access in the public market: the process was slow, and they didn't achieve the number. But Emmanuele also saw an opportunity. "Once in a while, we have unexpected results," she says. "Then we learn, and we go again."

1. Why do "achieved", "missed" and "presented" end in -ED?
A Because the action is FINISHED: last quarter is over. Most verbs take -ed in the past.
B Because the action is happening now, at this moment.
C Because the subject is "the team" (only one thing).
2. "Her team met sixty new doctors." Why is it NOT "meeted"?
A Because the verb is in the present.
B Because meet is one of the five irregular verbs of the business review: meet → met. Irregular verbs do NOT take -ed.
C Because the verb never changes after "team".
3. "They didn't achieve the number." Why is it NOT "didn't achieved"?
A Because the sentence is about the future.
B Because "achieve" is an irregular verb.
C Because the past is already inside did. After didn't, the verb goes back to normal — only ONE past mark in each sentence.
4. Which sentence about last quarter is correct?
A "Last quarter we exceed the target in dermatology."
B "Last quarter we didn't achieved the target in asthma."
C "Last quarter we exceeded the target in dermatology, and the growth came from the private market."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Saying what HAPPENED: -ed and the five irregular verbs

GRAMMAR

A result is always in the past. This is how English says what is finished.

StructureUseExample
Verb + -ed (regular verbs)The action is FINISHED. The quarter is over. The form is the SAME for I, you, we, they, he and she.We achieved the target. / She presented the plan.
The 5 irregular verbs of the business reviewThey do NOT take -ed: they become a new word. There are only five — learn them and you cover most of the meeting.go → went · have → had · give → gave · meet → met · come → came
Negative: didn't + NORMAL verbOnly ONE past mark in each sentence, and it is already inside did. So the verb goes back to normal.We didn't achieve the target. (never "didn't achieved")
When? (time expressions)last quarter · last week · in April · two months ago ("ago" comes AFTER the time, never before) · yesterday
Ready-made phrase: It was a difficult quarter.Nothing to build, nothing to change. It is ONE block: take it and use it.It was a good quarter for dermatology.

Careful (common mistake): in your language, the past is already inside the verb. That is why people say "we didn't achieved" — the past TWICE. In English, the past is inside did, and the verb goes back to normal: we didn't achieve. The opposite also happens: "last quarter" already says it is the past, so people forget the -ED and say "last quarter we exceed". In English, the VERB has to show it too: we exceeded.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Last quarter, we the target in dermatology."
"We the target in asthma."
"In May, my team sixty new doctors."
"The growth from the private market."
"We the target in the public market."
"Two months ago, we a challenge with market access."

Stage 2: Put the Quarter in Order

Order

Put the events of last quarter in the correct order.

?In May, my team met sixty new doctors in the private market.
?At the end of the quarter, we missed the target in asthma, because market access was slow.
?Last week, I presented the results to the Global team.
?In June, prescription volume grew and we exceeded the target in dermatology.
?In April, we started the quarter with a clear target.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of your August presentation. Watch the -ED: achieved = one sound; exceeded = one extra syllable.

Last quarter, we achieved our target in dermatology.
We missed the target in asthma, because market access was slow.
The growth came from the private market.
Once in a while, we have unexpected results.
Let me check the number and come back to you.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of the business review with the Global team.

Marc asks: "How was last quarter in dermatology?" You answer:
A "Last quarter we exceed the target."
B "Last quarter, we exceeded the target in dermatology. We achieved one hundred and eight percent."
C "Last quarter we are exceeding the target."
He asks: "Did you reach the target in asthma?" The best answer is:
A "No, we didn't achieved the target."
B "No, sorry, sorry. It is my fault."
C "No. We missed the target in asthma, because market access was slow."
He asks: "Where did the growth come from?" Which sentence is correct?
A "The growth came from the private market. My team met sixty new doctors."
B "The growth comed from the private market. My team meeted sixty new doctors."
C "The growth is come from the private market."
You want to say WHEN the challenge started (two months ago). You say:
A "Ago two months, market access was slow."
B "Two months ago, market access was slow."
C "Before two months, market access was slow."
The Global VP says the numbers go up and down every quarter. The natural, professional answer is:
A "Yes, sometimes my work is very bad."
B "Yes. Once in a while, we have unexpected results. Then we learn, and we go again."
C "Yes, I am unexpected results."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

The Global team gives you two minutes at the quarterly business review. Tell them what happened last quarter at Sanofi Brazil, in this order: first the target (what you achieved and what you exceeded), then the challenge (what you missed, and why — use "because"), and finally the opportunity (the new doctors, the private market, where the growth came from). Use the past: -ed verbs and your five irregulars (went, had, gave, met, came). Close with the expression of the lesson: "Once in a while, we have unexpected results." Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1Last quarter, we achieved our target in dermatology.
2We missed the target in asthma, because market access was slow.
3The growth came from the private market.
4Once in a while, we have unexpected results.
5Let me check the number and come back to you.
Lesson 04 -- Pre-class

Breaking the Ice -- Small Talk at a Global Event

The dinner before your presentation: how to open a conversation with a person you do not know, say in one sentence what you are doing NOW, and keep the conversation alive to the end. Key words: small talk, congress, colleague, executive, stakeholder, pipeline, to introduce, to network, currently, to look forward to. Structures: present continuous — am / is / are + verb-ING for what happens NOW (I am currently working on...), next to the present simple for what is always true (I work in immunology), plus the key question at the table: "What are you working on at the moment?" Expression of the lesson: "I am looking forward to working with your team."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words of the dinner table and the congress hallway — none of them is on a results slide.

Small talk -- easy, friendly conversation before the real business starts
"The dinner is two hours of small talk."
Congress -- a big professional event where doctors and companies meet
"I go to the immunology congress every year."
Colleague -- a person who works with you, in your company or in another one
"I met a colleague from the French team."
Executive -- a senior leader who makes the big decisions in a company
"The executives from Paris are at the dinner."
Stakeholder -- a person or a group with an interest in your project
"Doctors and payers are our main stakeholders."
Pipeline -- the new medicines a company is developing, before they reach the market
"We have three new products in the pipeline."
To introduce -- to say who you are, or to present one person to another
"Let me introduce myself: I am Emmanuele."
To network -- to meet new people at an event and build professional contacts
"I network at every congress."
Currently -- now, in this period -- not every day, but at this moment in your life
"I am currently working on the asthma launch."
To look forward to -- to feel happy about something that is coming
"I am looking forward to the congress."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Small talk
Congress
Colleague
Executive
Stakeholder
Pipeline
To introduce
To network
Currently
To look forward to

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is the first evening of the congress, and Emmanuele is at the dinner table. She works in immunology — that is her job, and it does not change. But tonight nobody asks about her job. An executive from Paris turns to her and asks: "What are you working on at the moment?"

Emmanuele breathes. "I am currently working on the immunology portfolio. Right now, my team is preparing a launch, and my field team is visiting the big centers." The executive smiles. "We are watching Brazil very closely this year," he says. "And I am looking at the pipeline for next year — two new molecules."

She is not selling anything tonight. She is networking: she is meeting colleagues and stakeholders from twenty-two countries. And when the conversation ends, she says the sentence she practiced: "I am looking forward to working with your team." Tomorrow, in the meeting room, they already know her name.

1. Why does she say "I am working on the portfolio" and not "I work on the portfolio"?
A Because it is the project of THIS moment — it started and it will finish. NOW, not always.
B Because the action finished last quarter.
C Because "portfolio" is a plural word.
2. In the same text she says "She works in immunology". Why does this sentence take NO -ING?
A Because it is a negative sentence.
B Because it is her job — always true, and it does not change next month. That is present simple.
C Because the subject is "she", and after "she" the -ING never comes.
3. "My team is preparing a launch." Why is it NOT "My team preparing a launch"?
A Because "team" is plural and needs "are".
B Because the structure ALWAYS has two pieces: am / is / are + verb-ING. Without the first piece, the sentence breaks.
C Because "prepare" is an irregular verb.
4. Which question is correct at the dinner table?
A "What you are working on at the moment?"
B "What are you working on at the moment?"
C "What are you work on at the moment?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- What you are doing NOW: am / is / are + verb-ING

GRAMMAR

At a business dinner, the question is about NOW, not about always. This is how English shows the now.

StructureUseExample
I am / she is / we are + verb-INGThe project of NOW: this week, this month, this year. It started and it will finish.I am working on the launch. / My team is preparing the congress.
Always TWO pieces, never oneYou need both: am + working. Take one piece away and the sentence breaks.We are launching a product. (never "we launching", never "we are launch")
Negative: am not / is not + verb-INGTo say what is NOT happening now.I am not working on that project this year.
Question: What are you working on?The question at the table. Learn it as one block — do not build it word by word. Note that are and you change places.What are you working on at the moment?
Always (present simple) x Now (-ING)I work in immunology = my job, it does not change · I am working on the asthma launch = this month, it will finish. The words that ask for the -ING: right now, currently, at the moment, this month.

Careful (a common mistake): in your language, one verb can say both things — the always and the now. In English it cannot. If you say "I work on the asthma launch" at the dinner, the executive hears that you do this every year and you always will. For the project of THIS month, English needs the two pieces: I am working on the asthma launch. The other mistake is to forget the first piece and say "I working on..." — in English, that is not a sentence.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form of the verb in parentheses. Careful: one of them is always true and takes NO -ING. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am currently on the immunology portfolio." (work)
"This month, my team the congress." (prepare)
"I in immunology." (work -- always true)
"We a new product in Brazil this month." (launch)
"What on at the moment?" (work)
"I to working with your team." (look forward)

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Put the dinner conversation in the correct order: from the introduction to the closing.

?And you? What are you working on at the moment?
?Hello. I do not think we have met. I am Emmanuele Orrico, from Sanofi Brazil.
?That is very interesting. I am looking forward to hearing more about it tomorrow.
?I am currently working on the immunology portfolio.
?It is great to finally meet you in person.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of the dinner. Careful: colleague has two syllables (KOL-eeg) and executive has the stress on the second one (eg-ZEK-yu-tiv).

Hello. I do not think we have met. I am Emmanuele, from Sanofi Brazil.
It is great to finally meet you in person.
I am currently working on the immunology portfolio.
And you? What are you working on at the moment?
I am looking forward to working with your team.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the dinner with the Global team.

You are alone in the coffee line. An executive you have never seen is next to you. You open the conversation with:
A "Excuse me, sorry, my English is very bad."
B "Hello. I do not think we have met. I am Emmanuele, from Sanofi Brazil."
C "Who are you and what is your function in the company?"
He asks: "What are you working on at the moment?" The best answer is:
A "I working on the immunology portfolio."
B "I am work on the immunology portfolio."
C "I am currently working on the immunology portfolio. Right now, my team is preparing a launch."
You want to say what is ALWAYS true — your area, your job title. You say:
A "I work in immunology. I am the National Demand Manager."
B "I am working in immunology since always."
C "I am work in immunology every day."
Now you want to ask him the same question. The correct form is:
A "And you? What you are working on?"
B "And you? What are you working on at the moment?"
C "And you? What are you work on?"
The conversation is ending and you want to meet this person again tomorrow. You close with:
A "Ok. Bye bye. Finish."
B "It was great to meet you. I am looking forward to working with your team."
C "I am looking forward to work with your team yesterday."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

It is August, and you are at the dinner with the Global team. An executive from Paris sits down next to you. Nobody introduces you — you start. In this order: (1) introduce yourself (name, company, and what you do — the things that are always true); (2) say what you are working on RIGHT NOW (use "I am currently working on..." and one sentence about what your team is preparing this month); (3) ask the other person what they are working on; (4) react to the answer ("That is very interesting"); and (5) close the conversation with the expression of the lesson: "I am looking forward to..." Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1Hello. I do not think we have met. I am Emmanuele, from Sanofi Brazil.
2It is great to finally meet you in person.
3I am currently working on the immunology portfolio.
4And you? What are you working on at the moment?
5I am looking forward to working with your team.
Lesson 05 -- Pre-class

What Are Your Plans? -- Launches, Meetings and Goals

Tell the Global team what comes next: the goal of the quarter, the strategy in the private market, the deadlines and the August visit to Brazil. Key words: business plan, goal, deadline, strategy, quarterly review, to announce, to organize, product approval, reimbursement, global alignment. Structures: going to for plans and intentions (I am going to present / we are going to announce), the negative (we are not going to launch) and the question (what are you going to do?); and the -ing form for what is already CONFIRMED in the calendar (we are meeting the Global team on August 24). Phrase of the lesson: "It depends on the market conditions."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words of your business plan.

Business plan -- the document with your goals and actions for the year
"I am going to send the business plan to Paris in April."
Goal -- the thing you want to reach in the future
"Our goal is the product approval in dermatology."
Deadline -- the last day to finish something
"The deadline for the business plan is April 30."
Strategy -- how you are going to reach the goal
"Our strategy is to work with the private hospitals."
Quarterly review -- the meeting every three months where each country presents the numbers
"I am going to present the numbers at the quarterly review."
To announce -- to tell everyone officially
"In May, we are going to announce the product approval."
To organize -- to plan and prepare the parts of an event or a meeting
"I am going to organize the visit to the hospitals."
Product approval -- when the agency says yes and doctors can prescribe the medicine
"The product approval is going to arrive in May."
Reimbursement -- when the health plan or the government pays for the medicine
"The reimbursement decision is going to change the plan."
Global alignment -- when Brazil and the Global team agree on the same plan
"We are going to work on global alignment before August."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Business plan
Goal
Deadline
Strategy
Quarterly review
To announce
To organize
Product approval
Reimbursement
Global alignment

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Paris wants the plan. In April, Emmanuele is going to send the business plan to Marc. Her goal is the product approval in dermatology, and in May the team is going to announce it. Her strategy is the private market: she is going to organize twenty new meetings with the hospitals. In June, she is going to present the numbers at the quarterly review. The reimbursement decision is going to arrive in July, but nobody is sure: "It depends on the market conditions," she says. The team is not going to launch in the public market this year. Before August, Brazil and Paris are going to work on global alignment. And the visit? That one is already in the calendar: they are meeting the Global team on August 24. The deadline for the document is April 30. "What are you going to present?" Marc asks. She knows the answer now.

1. Why is it "we are going to announce" and NOT "we are going to announcing"?
A Because "announce" is an irregular verb.
B Because after the going to block, the verb always comes back to its NORMAL form. Nothing changes in it.
C Because the sentence is in the past.
2. "They are meeting the Global team on August 24." Why does this sentence have NO "going to"?
A Because it is already CONFIRMED in the calendar, with a fixed date. A fixed appointment uses the -ing form.
B Because the action is happening right now, at this moment.
C Because the subject is plural ("they").
3. "What are you going to present?" Why is it NOT "What you are going to present?"
A Because in English every question starts with "what".
B Because "present" needs -ing in a question.
C Because in a question the first two words CHANGE places: "you are" → "are you".
4. Which sentence about the next quarter is correct?
A "I go to present the business plan at the quarterly review."
B "I am going to present the business plan at the quarterly review, and we are meeting the Global team in August."
C "I am going to presenting the business plan at the quarterly review."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Plan Block: GOING TO

GRAMMAR

A plan is always the future. This is how English says what is going to happen.

StructureUseExample
I am going to
We / You / They are going to
He / She / It is going to		A plan or an intention: you ALREADY DECIDED, but the date is not fixed yet. There is nothing to build — these are THREE ready blocks. Pick your block and go.We are going to focus on the private market.
+ verb in the NORMAL formAfter going to, the verb NEVER changes: no -ed, no -ing, no -s.going to announce · going to present · going to organize
Negative: not INSIDE the blockThe not goes in the middle of the block, never at the end of the sentence.We are not going to launch in the public market.
Question: the first two words change places"you are going to" becomes "are you going to".What are you going to present in August?
Already CONFIRMED in the calendar? Use the -ing form, with no "going to"The date is fixed, the ticket is booked, it is in your calendar. Simple rule: in your head = going to; in your calendar = -ing.We are meeting the Global team on August 24. · They are arriving on Sunday.
Ready phrase: It depends on the market conditions.Nothing to build here. It is the professional way to say "maybe" without sounding insecure — and without promising what is not in your hands."Is July safe?" — "It depends on the market conditions."

Common mistake: many people say "I go to present". That does NOT exist in English. The English block has three parts and you need all of them: I am going to present. And the other mistake, now that you know the -ING form from lesson 4: do not put -ING after the block. It is "we are going to launch", never "we are going to launching".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. Careful with the last one: that date is already in the calendar.

"I present the business plan in August."
"We focus on the private market this quarter."
"The reimbursement decision arrive in July."
"In May, we are going to the product approval."
"We launch in the public market this year."
"We the Global team on August 24. The date is confirmed."

Stage 2: Put the Next Quarter in Order

Order

Put the events of the next quarter in the correct order, from April to August.

?In April, I am going to send the business plan to Paris.
?In May, we are going to announce the product approval in dermatology.
?On August 24, we are having dinner with the global team in Brazil.
?In June, I am going to present the numbers at the quarterly review.
?In July, the reimbursement decision is going to arrive.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of the August plan. Careful with the stress: STRA-te-gy — the strong part is the first one.

Our goal is the product approval in dermatology.
We are going to focus on the private market this quarter.
I am going to present the business plan in August.
It depends on the market conditions.
Let me send you the plan before the deadline.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of the planning call with the Global team.

Marc asks: "What are your plans for the next quarter?" You answer:
A "We go to focus on the private market."
B "Our goal is the product approval in dermatology. We are going to announce it in May."
C "Last quarter we exceeded the target in dermatology."
He asks: "And what is the strategy?" The best answer is:
A "We are going to focusing on the private hospitals."
B "We are going to focus on the private hospitals. I am going to organize twenty new meetings."
C "I organize twenty meetings yesterday."
The Global VP asks if the reimbursement decision arrives in July. You CANNOT promise it. You say:
A "Yes, yes, no problem, July, sure."
B "I do not know. Sorry."
C "The decision is going to arrive in July, but it depends on the market conditions."
The dinner with the Global team on August 24 is ALREADY confirmed in the calendar. You say:
A "We are having dinner with the global team on August 24."
B "We have dinner with the global team every August 24."
C "We had dinner with the global team on August 24."
You want to ASK Marc what he is going to present on the visit. You say:
A "What you are going to present in Brazil?"
B "What are you going to present in Brazil?"
C "What are you going to presenting in Brazil?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

Marc Dubois gives you two minutes on the planning call. Tell him the plan for the next quarter at Sanofi Brazil, in this order: first the goal (what you are going to achieve, and when you are going to announce it), then the strategy (where you are going to focus, and what you are going to organize), and finally the deadlines (the business plan, the reimbursement decision). Say one thing that is already confirmed in the calendar, using the -ing form: "We are meeting the Global team on August 24." And when he asks about a date you cannot promise, use the expression of the lesson: "It depends on the market conditions." Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1Our goal is the product approval in dermatology.
2We are going to focus on the private market this quarter.
3I am going to present the business plan in August.
4It depends on the market conditions.
5Let me send you the plan before the deadline.
Lesson 06 -- Pre-class

How Is It Going? -- Reporting Progress to the Global Team

The check-in in the middle of the road: Marc already has the PLAN, now he wants the PROGRESS. How many centers are on track, how much real progress there is, what the delays are — and how to say all of that in three lines, without saying sorry. Key words: campaign, milestone, update, progress, to roll out, to track, on track, ongoing, to focus on, to discuss. Structures: how many x how much, and the quantifiers a lot of, a few, a little, some and any — one question decides all of them: can you count it? Expression of the lesson: "So far, so good."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words of a progress report — the words the Global team wants to hear on a Friday call.

Campaign -- an organized set of actions to bring a medicine to doctors
"The dermatology campaign is on track in eleven centers."
Milestone -- an important point in a project, where you check if you are on time
"The first milestone is complete."
Update -- new information about the situation of a project
"Here is a short update on the campaign."
Progress -- the movement forward in a project
"We have a lot of progress in the private market."
To roll out -- to start something step by step, in more and more places
"We are rolling it out in four more centers this month."
To track -- to follow the numbers of a project, to see where it is
"My team is tracking the delays in the public market."
On track -- going well -- at the right speed, at the right time
"The campaign is on track in eleven centers."
Ongoing -- happening now, and not finished yet
"The roll-out is ongoing in four centers."
To focus on -- to give your attention and your time to one thing
"This month we are focusing on the private market."
To discuss -- to talk about something with other people, to make a decision
"On Friday we are going to discuss the delays."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Campaign
Milestone
Update
Progress
To roll out
To track
On track
Ongoing
To focus on
To discuss

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is Friday, and Marc is on the call. He already has the plan — today he wants the progress. "So, how is it going?" he asks. Emmanuele opens her update. "The first milestone is complete, and the dermatology campaign is on track."

"How many centers are active?" — "Eleven. And we are rolling it out in four more this month." Marc writes the number down. "And how much progress is there in the private market?" — "A lot of progress," she says. "This month we are focusing on the big hospitals."

Then the difficult part, and she does not apologize for it. "We have a few delays in the public market. There is not much information about the reimbursement yet, so my team is tracking it every week. The roll-out there is still ongoing." Marc nods. "Are there any problems you cannot solve alone?" — "Not yet. So far, so good. But on Friday I would like to discuss the delays with you."

1. Why does Marc ask "How many centers" and not "How much centers"?
A Because you can COUNT them: one center, two centers, three centers. Things you can count take how many.
B Because "centers" is a short word.
C Because it is a question, and every question uses "how many".
2. And why does he ask "How much progress" in the next line?
A Because "progress" is already a plural word.
B Because you CANNOT count it: nobody says "one progress, two progresses". Things you cannot count take how much.
C Because the question is about money.
3. She says "We have a few delays" and "there is not much information". Why are the two words different?
A Because "delays" is a bad thing and "information" is a good thing.
B Because the first sentence is affirmative and the second one is negative — only for that.
C Because you can count delays (one delay, two delays) → a few. And you cannot count information → much.
4. Which update is correct?
A "We have many progress, and there is not many information about the reimbursement."
B "We have a lot of progress, and there is not much information about the reimbursement."
C "We have much progresses, and there are not much informations about the reimbursement."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- One question decides everything: can you count it?

GRAMMAR

Before you choose the word, count in your head: one center, two centers — yes, you can count it. One progress, two progresses — no, that does not exist. This one question gives you the answer every time.

StructureUseExample
how many · a fewFor things you CAN count: 1 center, 2 centers, 3 centers.How many centers are on track? · We have a few delays.
how much · a littleFor things you CANNOT count. You never say "two progresses".How much progress is there? · We have a little time.
a lot ofIt works for BOTH — your safety net. When you are not sure, use this one.a lot of centers · a lot of progress
some / anysome = you HAVE it. any = you ASK, or you do NOT have it.We have some good news. · Are there any updates? · There is not any news yet.
The list to learn by heartYou never count these words in English: progress, information, news, time, work, feedback. They have no plural, and they never take "a": never "an information", never "two progresses", never "many feedbacks".

Common mistake: "many progress" and "how much centers" — both are wrong, and both for the same reason: the wrong word for the wrong side of the question. The quick fix: count first. One center, two centers → you can count it → many / a few. One progress, two progresses? → that does not exist → much / a little. And the most expensive mistake of all: "an information". In English it does NOT exist. It is always some information — and the same goes for news ("The news is good", never "the news are").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Before you write, ask the question: can you count it? Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"How centers are active?"
"How progress is there in the private market?"
"We have progress this month."
"We have delays in the public market."
"There is not information about the reimbursement yet."
"Are there updates from your side?"

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Put the update in the correct order, from the opening to the closing. Look at the professional pattern: good news first, problem after, closing at the end.

?So far, so good.
?We have a lot of progress in the private market: eleven centers are active.
?The first milestone is complete, and we are on track.
?But we have a few delays in the public market, because there is not much information about the reimbursement yet.
?Good morning, Marc. Here is the update on the dermatology campaign.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of the Friday update. Note the stress: campaign is strong on the second part (kam-PEIN), and progress, as a noun, is strong on the first part (PRO-gres).

Here is a short update on the dermatology campaign.
The first milestone is complete, and we are on track.
We have a lot of progress in the private market.
We have a few delays, and there is not much information yet.
So far, so good.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the call with the Global team.

Marc opens the call: "So, how is it going?" The best way to start is:
A "Sorry, I have a lot of problems and my English is not good."
B "Here is a short update on the dermatology campaign. The first milestone is complete, and we are on track."
C "I am going to send you forty slides tomorrow."
He asks how many centers are active. You answer:
A "There is much centers active."
B "Eleven centers are active, and we are rolling it out in four more this month."
C "We have a little centers active."
You want to say there is a LOT of progress in the private market. The safe way is:
A "We have a lot of progress in the private market."
B "We have many progress in the private market."
C "We have many progresses in the private market."
Now the difficult part: there are two or three delays, and almost no information about the reimbursement. You report:
A "We have a little delays and there is not many information."
B "We have a few delays in the public market, and there is not much information about the reimbursement yet."
C "I am very sorry, we have many delays and I do not have an information."
Before you close the call, you want to know if he has news. You ask:
A "Do you have some updates from your side?"
B "Are there any updates from your side?"
C "Are there many update from your side?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

It is Friday, and Marc Dubois opens the call with one question: "So, how is it going?" Give him the update on a REAL project of yours (atopic dermatitis, asthma or COPD), in this order: (1) open it -- "Here is a short update on..."; (2) the good news, and the things you can COUNT (how many centers are active, how many doctors your team is visiting, how many milestones are complete); (3) the things you CANNOT count (how much progress there is in the market, how much time you still have, how much information is still missing); (4) the problems -- say them honestly, and do not apologize for them; (5) ask him one question back ("Are there any updates from your side?"); and (6) close with the expression of the lesson: "So far, so good." Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1Here is a short update on the dermatology campaign.
2The first milestone is complete, and we are on track.
3We have a lot of progress in the private market.
4We have a few delays, and there is not much information yet.
5So far, so good.
Lesson 07 -- Pre-class

What's Next? -- Next Steps and Offers in the Global Meeting

Close the Global meeting like a leader: give the next steps you already planned, and make offers on the spot without freezing. Key words: agenda, next steps, outcome, to follow up, to commit, to confirm, offer, to arrange, upcoming. Structures: will ('ll) for decisions, offers and promises you make in the moment (I'll send it right now / I'll get back to you), the negative (I won't be late) and the question (will you send it?); in contrast with going to for the next steps you planned before (I am going to follow up with the hospitals). Phrase of the lesson: "I'll check and get back to you."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the nine words you hear at the end of every meeting.

Agenda -- the list of topics for a meeting
"Let's look at the agenda for the Global meeting."
Next steps -- the actions you take after the meeting
"Let me tell you the next steps for the project."
Outcome -- the final result of a meeting or a decision
"The outcome of the meeting is a new plan for Brazil."
To follow up -- to check something again later, after the meeting
"I am going to follow up with the hospital next week."
To commit -- to promise you are going to do something
"I commit to send the report on Friday."
To confirm -- to say that something is certain
"I will confirm the dates with Paris tomorrow."
Offer -- when you say you will help or do something for someone
"Thank you for the offer. That is very kind."
To arrange -- to plan and organize a time or a meeting
"I will arrange a call with the field team."
Upcoming -- happening soon, in the near future
"The upcoming meeting is very important for Brazil."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Agenda
Next steps
Outcome
To follow up
To commit
To confirm
Offer
To arrange
Upcoming

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The meeting is ending, and Marc wants the next steps for Brazil. Emmanuele is ready. "Let me give you the next steps," she says. This week she is going to send the agenda, and next week she is going to follow up with the private hospitals. Then Marc asks for the Brazil report today. She does not freeze. "No problem, I'll send it right now," she says. The dates for the August visit are not confirmed yet, so she adds: "I'll confirm them tomorrow and I'll get back to you." Marc offers to prepare a template. "Thank you for the offer," she answers. The outcome is clear, and the upcoming meeting has an agenda. She commits to send everything before Friday.

1. Why is it "I'll send it right now" and NOT "I am going to send it"?
A She decides it in THIS moment — it is an offer on the spot. will / 'll is for a decision made now.
B Because "send" is an irregular verb.
C Because the sentence is about the past.
2. "I will to send the report." Why is this WRONG?
A Because after will there is no "to". The correct form is "I'll send the report".
B Because "report" needs a capital letter.
C Because you must always say "going to".
3. "Next week she is going to follow up with the hospitals." Why going to here?
A Because it is a plan she made BEFORE the meeting, not a decision in the moment.
B Because "follow up" cannot be used with will.
C Because the action is happening right now.
4. Which sentence is correct at the end of a meeting?
A "You need it now? I'll send it right now, and next week I am going to follow up with the hospitals."
B "You need it now? I will sending it, and next week I go to follow up."
C "You need it now? I send it, and next week I will to follow up."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Offer Block: WILL

GRAMMAR

A meeting ends with two things: the steps you planned (going to) and the yes you give on the spot (will). This is the yes.

StructureUseExample
I'll · We'll · You'll
They'll · He'll · She'll
(= I will, we will...)		A decision, an offer or a promise you make in this moment — you did NOT plan it before. One ready block for everyone.I'll send it right now.
+ verb in the NORMAL formAfter will / 'll, the verb NEVER changes: no "to", no -ing, no -ed, no -s.I'll send · I'll confirm · I'll check
Negative: won't (= will not)One short block. It is the opposite of the offer: what you will not do.I won't be late for the meeting.
Question: Will you...?Put will at the front, then the person.Will you send me the agenda?
going to = planned before · will = decided nowThe next steps you planned use going to. The offer you give on the spot uses will. Simple test: did you plan it before, or decide it now?I am going to follow up next week. · "You need it now? I'll send it."
Ready phrase: I'll get back to you.Nothing to build. The professional way to say "I'll answer soon" when you cannot say yes yet — instead of freezing or promising wrong."Is July safe?" — "I'll check and get back to you."

Common mistake: many people say "I will to send" — that "to" does NOT exist after will. It is "I'll send". And do not put -ING after it: "I'll confirm", never "I will confirming". For a quick offer, always use I'll, never the simple present ("I send it").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence. Careful: an offer on the spot uses will; a plan you made before uses going to.

"You need the report today? I send it right now."
"Don't worry about the dates. I confirm them tomorrow."
"Next week, I follow up with the private hospitals."
"After will, the verb stays normal: I'll you the report."
"I am not sure yet, but I get back to you before Friday."
"This week, we send the agenda for the upcoming meeting."

Stage 2: Put the Close in Order

Order

Put the end of the meeting in the correct order, from the first sentence to the last.

?Thank you all for the meeting. Let me give you the next steps.
?First, I am going to send the agenda to Paris this week.
?And I'll get back to you with the final outcome before Friday.
?Then I'll confirm the dates for the August visit.
?After that, I am going to follow up with the private hospitals.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences to close any meeting. Careful with the linking: "I'll send" is fast — one sound, not two words.

Let me give you the next steps.
I'll send you the report right now.
I am going to follow up with the hospitals next week.
I'll check and get back to you before Friday.
The outcome is a clear plan for the next quarter.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment at the end of the Global meeting.

Marc asks: "So, what are your next steps?" You answer:
A "Let me give you the next steps. This week, I am going to send the agenda."
B "I go to send the agenda this week."
C "Last week I sent the agenda."
Marc says: "I need the Brazil report today." You answer:
A "No problem. I'll send it right now."
B "I will to send it right now."
C "I am going to think about it."
He asks about the August dates. You cannot confirm them yet. You say:
A "I'll check with my team and get back to you tomorrow."
B "Yes, yes, sure, no problem, confirmed."
C "I do not know. Sorry."
Claire offers to prepare a slide template for you. You answer:
A "Thank you for the offer. That is very kind."
B "No, I do the template alone always."
C "Thank you for the offering."
You want to ASK Marc if he will send the final agenda. You say:
A "Will you send me the final agenda?"
B "You will send me the final agenda?"
C "Will you sending me the final agenda?"

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

You are closing a Global meeting with Marc Dubois. Do it in this order: first give your next steps (two or three things you are going to do, and when), using "going to"; then make two offers on the spot with "I'll" (for example, send the report now, or confirm a date); and when he asks about something you cannot promise yet, use the phrase of the lesson: "I'll check and get back to you." Finish with the outcome: "The outcome is a clear plan for the next quarter." Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1Let me give you the next steps.
2I'll send you the report right now.
3I am going to follow up with the hospitals next week.
4I'll check and get back to you before Friday.
5The outcome is a clear plan for the next quarter.
Lesson 08 -- Pre-class

Better, Bigger, Best -- Comparing Results in the Business Review

The business review: not one number, but a comparison. How Brazil compares to the region — bigger or smaller than Mexico, higher or lower growth — which area has the best performance, and where the biggest gap is. Key words: performance, market share, figure, trend, gap, improvement, leader, region, priority, significant. Structures: comparatives (bigger, higher, better than) and superlatives (the biggest, the most important, the best) — one question decides all of them: is the word short or long? Expression of the lesson: "In my opinion..."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the ten words of a business review — the words you compare with when you present to the Global team.

Performance -- how well a product, a team or a market works and sells
"This quarter, asthma has the best performance."
Market share -- the part of the whole market that one company has
"Our market share in dermatology is bigger than last year."
Figure -- a number in a report or a chart
"Twelve thousand is a big figure for this market."
Trend -- the way the numbers change over time, up or down
"The trend is very positive this year."
Gap -- the difference, or the empty space, between two numbers
"There is a significant gap between the public and the private market."
Improvement -- when something becomes better than before
"We see a clear improvement in the private market."
Leader -- the product, team or country that is number one
"Brazil is the leader in Latin America."
Region -- a large area, like a group of countries
"Latin America is the region with the highest growth."
Priority -- the most important thing, the thing you do first
"Atopic dermatitis is our most important priority."
Significant -- big and important, not small
"This is a significant trend for next year."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Performance
Market share
Figure
Trend
Gap
Improvement
Leader
Region
Priority
Significant

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is the business review, and Marc is on the call. He does not want one figure alone — he wants a comparison. "So, how does Brazil compare to the region?" he asks. Emmanuele opens her scorecard. "In my opinion, Brazil is the leader in Latin America. It is the biggest market, and this year it has the best performance."

"Bigger than Mexico?" — "Yes, bigger than Mexico. But Mexico has a higher growth rate, so the gap between us is smaller than last year." Marc writes it down. "And your most important priority?" — "Atopic dermatitis. The trend is very positive: the numbers are better than last quarter, and it is our biggest opportunity."

Then the difficult part, and she does not hide it. "Our biggest challenge is the public market share. There is a significant gap there. But in my opinion, it is also the best opportunity for next year, because the improvement can be the fastest of all." Marc nods. Bigger is not always better — and she just proved she knows it.

1. Why does Emmanuele say "the biggest market" and not "the most big market"?
A Because "big" is a SHORT word (one syllable), so it changes its end: big → bigger → the biggest.
B Because "market" is a big place.
C Because every superlative in English uses "the most".
2. And why does she say "the most important priority" and not "the importantest priority"?
A Because "priority" is a plural word.
B Because "important" is a LONG word (three syllables), so it takes "the most": the most important.
C Because the sentence is a question.
3. She says the numbers are "better than last quarter". Why "better", and not "gooder"?
A Because "good" is a short word, so it becomes "gooder".
B Because after a number you always say "better".
C Because "good" is irregular: good → better → the best. You learn it by heart. And a comparison always ends with "than".
4. Which comparison is correct?
A "Brazil is more big that Mexico, and it has the most best performance."
B "Brazil is bigger than Mexico, and it has the best performance."
C "Brazil is the bigger than Mexico, and it has more good performance."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- One question decides everything: is the word short or long?

GRAMMAR

Before you compare, count the beats of the adjective. big — one beat, a short word: it changes its end (bigger, the biggest). im-por-tant — three beats, a long word: it takes a helper (more important, the most important). This one question gives you the answer every time.

Type of wordComparative (two things) + thanSuperlative (number one) + the
SHORT word (1 syllable): big, high, low, fast, smalladd -er: bigger than, higher thanthe + -est: the biggest, the highest
LONG word (2+ syllables): important, significant, expensivemore + word: more important thanthe most + word: the most important
IRREGULAR (learn by heart)good → better than · bad → worse thangood → the best · bad → the worst
The two rules you must never breakA comparison always ends with than (never "that"). A superlative always starts with the. And never both roads at once: never "more bigger", never "the most biggest".

Common mistake: "more big" and "the more important" — both are wrong. In your first language one word can work for every case, so it feels natural to put "more" in front of everything. But English has two roads: short words change their end (big → bigger), long words take a helper (important → more important). Never mix them, and never use both (never "more bigger"). And the smallest, most expensive mistake of all: "than" vs "that". A comparison finishes with than — "bigger than Mexico" — never "bigger that Mexico".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Before you write, ask the question: is the word short or long? The base word is in brackets. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Brazil is than Mexico. (big)"
"Atopic dermatitis is the priority. (important)"
"Mexico has a growth rate than Brazil. (high)"
"Asthma has the performance this quarter. (good)"
"This is the opportunity in the region. (big)"
"In my opinion, this is the trend. (significant)"

Stage 2: Put the Review in Order

Order

Put the comparison in the correct order, from the opening to the closing opinion. Look at the professional pattern: open, compare the size, compare the growth, name the priority, close with your opinion.

?Mexico has a higher growth rate, but the gap is smaller than last year.
?In my opinion, that is the most significant trend of the quarter.
?Brazil is the biggest market in Latin America, and this year it has the best performance.
?Our most important priority is atopic dermatitis, the biggest opportunity.
?Good morning, Marc. Here is the comparison for Brazil.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of the business review. Note the stress: performance is strong on the second part (per-FOR-mans), and significant is strong on the second part too (sig-NIF-i-cant).

Brazil is the biggest market in Latin America.
Mexico has a higher growth rate, but the gap is smaller than last year.
Atopic dermatitis is our most important priority.
Asthma has the best performance this quarter.
In my opinion, this is the most significant trend.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the business review with the Global team.

Marc asks: "How does Brazil compare to Mexico in size?" You answer:
A "Brazil is more big than Mexico."
B "Brazil is bigger than Mexico — it is the biggest market in Latin America."
C "Brazil is the bigger that Mexico."
He asks about the growth rate. Mexico is growing faster. You say:
A "Mexico has a more high growth rate."
B "Mexico has a higher growth rate, but the gap is smaller than last year."
C "Mexico is the most fast in growth."
You want to name your number-one priority. The best way is:
A "Atopic dermatitis is our most important priority this quarter."
B "Atopic dermatitis is our importantest priority this quarter."
C "Atopic dermatitis is our the more important priority."
Marc asks which area performs best. Asthma is number one. You report:
A "Asthma has the most best performance."
B "Asthma has the gooder performance of the three."
C "Asthma has the best performance of the three areas."
You want to give your opinion about the trend, with authority but not arrogance. You say:
A "This is the most significant trend, and I am 100% sure of everything."
B "In my opinion, this is the most significant trend for next year."
C "This trend is the more significant of all, believe me."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

It is the business review, and Marc Dubois asks one question: "How does Brazil compare to the region?" Present a comparison of a REAL part of your work (Brazil vs another country, or two therapeutic areas -- atopic dermatitis, asthma, COPD), in this order: (1) open with your opinion -- "In my opinion, Brazil is the leader / the biggest market..."; (2) compare the size and the growth -- who is bigger, who has a higher rate, and how the gap is changing; (3) name your most important priority and the area with the best performance; (4) point to the biggest gap or challenge, and turn it into the best opportunity for next year; (5) close with the most significant trend. Use at least five comparatives or superlatives. Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1Brazil is the biggest market in Latin America.
2Mexico has a higher growth rate, but the gap is smaller than last year.
3Atopic dermatitis is our most important priority.
4Asthma has the best performance this quarter.
5In my opinion, this is the most significant trend.
Lesson 09 -- Pre-class

Networking in English -- Starting and Keeping a Conversation

Open, keep and close a conversation at a global event: the welcome reception, the conference dinner and the moment to exchange contacts. Key words: conference, panel, speaker, session, to catch up, to mention, to break the ice, once in a while. This lesson has no new grammar: it is a review that puts together the present simple (I work), the present continuous (right now we are launching) and going to (I am going to present) inside one real conversation. The flow of the lesson: Open → Share → Ask → Bridge → Close. Opener of the lesson: "Have you been to this conference before?"
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of the room.

Conference -- a big professional event with talks and meetings
"The immunology conference is in São Paulo this year."
Panel -- a group of experts who discuss a topic on a stage
"She is going to speak on the panel about market access."
Speaker -- the person who gives a talk at an event
"The main speaker is a doctor from Paris."
Session -- one part of an event, with one topic
"The morning session is about new treatments."
To catch up -- to talk with someone you have not seen for a while
"Let's catch up at the dinner tonight."
To mention -- to say something quickly, not as the main point
"She mentioned the new study during the coffee break."
To break the ice -- to start a conversation and make people relax
"A simple question is a good way to break the ice."
Once in a while -- sometimes, but not often
"We meet the Global team once in a while."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Conference
Panel
Speaker
Session
To catch up
To mention
To break the ice
Once in a while

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The conference dinner starts at seven. Emmanuele does not know many people, so she decides to break the ice. She goes to a man near the window. "Hi, I'm Emmanuele. Have you been to this conference before?" she says. The man is from the Global team. They start to talk. "What do you do?" he asks. "I work at Sanofi Brazil. I am the national demand manager," she answers. "Right now, we are launching a new product in dermatology." She also mentions the immunology panel from the afternoon session.
