"And in August, I am going to present the results to the Global team," she adds. "What about you?" They only see each other once in a while, so it is good to catch up. At the end, she says: "It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch."

1. "I work at Sanofi Brazil." Why is it "work" and not "am working"?
A Because it is the past.
B Because it is her JOB — a fact that is always true. For your job, use the present simple.
C Because "work" is an irregular verb.
2. "Right now, we are launching a new product." Why the -ing form here?
A Because it is happening NOW, in this period. "Right now" is the signal.
B Because it is a plan for next year.
C Because "we" is plural.
3. "What do you do?" Why is there a "do" in the question?
A Because every English question needs "what".
B Because a present simple question needs "do": "What do you do?", never "What you do?".
C Because the verb "do" is repeated for style.
4. Which line is the best way to CLOSE the conversation?
A "Ok, bye."
B "It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch."
C "I go now."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Three Times, One Conversation

GRAMMAR

No new grammar today. This is a review: in one real conversation, the three times you already know live together.

TimeUse it forExample
Present simple
(I work / I am)		Your job and facts that are always true. In a question, add do: "What do you do?"I work in immunology. I am the national demand manager.
Present continuous
(we are launching)		What is happening NOW, in this period. Signal words: right now, at the moment.Right now, we are launching a new product.
Going to
(I am going to present)		A plan for the future. You already decided it.In August, I am going to present the results.
Ready opener: Have you been to this conference before?One block to break the ice. Do not build it — use it whole, every time."Have you been to this conference before?"

The flow: Open → Share → Ask → Bridge → Close. You do not improvise the whole conversation — you just take the next step. The line that helps you most is the bridge: "What about you?" — it gives the ball back and takes the weight off you.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Hi, I'm Emmanuele. you been to this conference before?"
"I at Sanofi Brazil in immunology."
"Right now, we a new product in dermatology."
"In August, I present the results."
"What you do at Sanofi?"
"It was great to . Let's stay in touch."

Stage 2: Put the Conversation in Order

Order

Put the lines of a networking conversation in the correct order, from the first hello to the goodbye.

?Hi, I'm Emmanuele. Nice to meet you.
?I work at Sanofi Brazil.
?It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch.
?Have you been to this conference before?
?Right now, we are launching a new product in dermatology.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These are the five lines that carry a whole conversation. Careful with KON-fe-rence — the strong part is the first one.

Hi, I'm Emmanuele. Nice to meet you.
Have you been to this conference before?
What brings you here?
Right now, we are launching a new product in dermatology.
It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of the conversation at the Global event.

You are next to someone new at the coffee break. You want to break the ice. You say:
A (You say nothing and look at your phone.)
B "Hi, I'm Emmanuele. Have you been to this conference before?"
C "Who are you?"
The person asks: "What do you do?" The best answer is:
A "I working in immunology."
B "I'm the national demand manager at Sanofi Brazil."
C "I go to present in August."
You want to talk about your current project. You say:
A "Right now, we are launching a new product in dermatology."
B "We launch a new product yesterday."
C "We are going to launched a new product."
You have talked for a while, and you want to give the ball back to the other person. You say:
A "That's all about me."
B "What about you?"
C "Are you finish?"
The conversation is ending and you want to close it warmly. You say:
A "Ok. Bye."
B "I have to go now, sorry."
C "It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

You are at the conference dinner, next to a colleague from the Global team you do not know. Have the whole conversation, out loud, in five steps: OPEN (break the ice with a question), SHARE (your name, your company, your job), ASK (a question to the other person), BRIDGE (say your current project and then "What about you?"), and CLOSE (end warmly: "It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch."). Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1Hi, I'm Emmanuele. Nice to meet you.
2Have you been to this conference before?
3What brings you here?
4Right now, we are launching a new product in dermatology.
5It was great to catch up. Let's stay in touch.
Lesson 10 -- Pre-class

How Much and How Many? -- Talking About Patients, Data and Market Access

The last lesson of the block: the numbers Global always asks for. How many patients can take the treatment, how much evidence there is for the dose, how much access there is in the public sector — and how to say what changed. Key words: patient population, coverage, funding, data, evidence, dose, treatment. Structures: how many x how much, the quantifiers a lot of, a few, several, a little and not much — one question decides them all: can you count it? — and the chunk used to for the past that is not true today.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the seven words of market access — the words Global uses when they ask about your numbers.

Patient population -- all the people who have a disease and can take a treatment
"The patient population is large, and it is growing."
Coverage -- how much a health plan or the public system pays for a treatment
"Several new programs give better coverage in the private sector."
Funding -- the money that pays for a program or a treatment
"There is not much public funding yet."
Data -- the numbers and facts about results
"We have more data every quarter."
Evidence -- facts and studies that show a treatment works
"We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose."
Dose -- the amount of a medicine a patient takes at one time
"There is a lot of evidence for the new dose."
Treatment -- the medical care and medicine a doctor gives to a patient
"The patient population for the dermatology treatment is large."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Patient population
Coverage
Funding
Data
Evidence
Dose
Treatment

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is the business review, and Marc wants the numbers. "So, how many patients can take this treatment?" he asks. "The patient population is large, and it is growing," Emmanuele answers. "And how much evidence do we have for the higher dose?" — "A lot of new evidence, and more data every quarter."

Then the access. "In the private sector there are several new programs, so coverage is better." Then the difficult part, and she does not apologize for it. "In the public sector there is not much funding yet, and the reimbursement decision is slow."

Marc nods. "And two years ago?" — "Two years ago there used to be almost no access, so this is real progress. Patients used to wait a long time. Are there any updates on the reimbursement from your side?"

1. Why does Marc ask "How many patients" and not "How much patients"?
A Because you can COUNT them: one patient, two patients, three patients. Things you can count take how many.
B Because "patients" is a short word.
C Because it is a question, and every question uses "how many".
2. And why does he ask "How much evidence" in the next line?
A Because "evidence" is already a plural word.
B Because you CANNOT count it: nobody says "one evidence, two evidences". Things you cannot count take how much.
C Because the question is about money.
3. She says "several new programs" and "not much funding". Why are the two words different?
A Because "programs" is a good thing and "funding" is a bad thing.
B Because the first sentence is affirmative and the second one is negative — only for that.
C Because you can count programs (one program, two programs) → several. And you cannot count funding → much.
4. What does "used to" tell us in "there used to be almost no access"?
A That there is still almost no access today.
B That it was true in the past, but it is NOT true today — the access is better now.
C That she uses the access every day.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- One question decides everything: can you count it?

GRAMMAR

Before you choose the word, count in your head: one patient, two patients — yes, you can count it. One evidence, two evidences — no, that does not exist. And to compare the past with today, one small chunk does the work: used to.

StructureUseExample
how many · a few · severalFor things you CAN count: 1 patient, 2 patients, 3 programs.How many patients? · We have several new programs.
how much · a littleFor things you CANNOT count. You never say "two evidences".How much evidence? · We have a little funding.
a lot ofIt works for BOTH — your safety net. When you are not sure, use this one.a lot of patients · a lot of evidence
used to + verbThe past that is NOT true today. The verb stays in its base form (used to be, used to wait).There used to be little access. · Patients used to wait a long time.
The list to learn by heartYou never count these words in English: data, evidence, access, funding, coverage, information. They have no plural, and they never take "a": never "an evidence", never "two datas".

Common mistake: "many evidence" and "how much patients" — both are wrong, and both for the same reason: the wrong word for the wrong side of the question. The quick fix: count first. One patient, two patients → you can count it → many / several. One evidence, two evidences? → that does not exist → much / a little. And after used to, the verb stays in its base form: used to be, never "used to been".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Before you write, ask the question: can you count it? Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"How patients can take this treatment?"
"How evidence do we have for the dose?"
"We have new data this quarter."
"There are new programs in the private sector."
"There is not public funding yet."
"Two years ago there be almost no access."

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Put the update in the correct order, from the opening to the closing. Look at the professional pattern: good news first, problem after, the change at the end.

?We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose, and several new programs in the private sector.
?But there is not much public funding yet, and the reimbursement decision is slow.
?Good morning, Marc. Here is the market access update for Brazil.
?Two years ago there used to be almost no access, so this is real progress.
?The patient population is large, and it is growing.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five sentences of the data update. Note: data is DAY-ta (American), evidence is strong on the first part (E-vi-dence), and used to sounds like "USE-to".

The patient population is large, and it is growing.
We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose.
There are several new programs in the private sector.
There is not much public funding yet.
Two years ago there used to be almost no access.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of the business review with the Global team.

Marc asks: "How many patients can take this treatment?" You answer:
A "There is much patients in Brazil."
B "The patient population is large, and it is growing."
C "We have a little patients who can take it."
He asks how much evidence there is for the higher dose. The safe way is:
A "We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose."
B "We have many evidence for the higher dose."
C "We have many evidences for the higher dose."
You want to report the new private-sector programs, which you can count. You say:
A "There is much programs in the private sector."
B "There are several new programs in the private sector."
C "There is a little programs in the private sector."
Now the difficult part: almost no money in the public sector. You report:
A "I am very sorry, there is not many funding."
B "There is not much public funding yet, and the decision is slow."
C "There are not much fundings in the public sector."
You want to compare today with two years ago. You say:
A "Two years ago there used to been almost no access."
B "Two years ago there used to be almost no access, so this is real progress."
C "Two years ago there use to be almost no access."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

Marc Dubois asks you one question in the business review: "Can you tell me about patient access for your main products in Brazil?" Give him the picture for a REAL product of yours (atopic dermatitis, asthma or COPD), in this order: (1) open it -- "Here is the market access update for..."; (2) the things you can COUNT (how many patients, how many programs); (3) the things you CANNOT count (how much evidence, how much funding, how much access); (4) the problems -- say them honestly, and do not apologize for them; (5) compare today with the past using "used to" ("Two years ago there used to be..."); and (6) ask him one question back ("Are there any updates on the reimbursement?"). Use at least four quantifiers (a lot of, several, a few, not much). Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1The patient population is large, and it is growing.
2We have a lot of new evidence for the higher dose.
3There are several new programs in the private sector.
4There is not much public funding yet.
5Two years ago there used to be almost no access.
Lesson 11 -- Pre-class

What's Happening Right Now -- Describing Live Situations

Give a live status to the Global team: what your team is doing right now in Brazil -- the launch, the field and the results, in real time. Key words: to monitor, to adjust, to handle, situation, on the ground, in real time, status, to run. The grammar of the lesson is the present continuous (am/is/are + verb-ing) for what is happening now, in contrast with the present simple for your routine: "We visit doctors every week" vs "This week, we are visiting doctors in São Paulo." This is a consolidation of the -ing form you already met in lessons 4 and 6. Signal words: right now, at the moment, this week.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of a live update.

To monitor -- to watch and check something carefully over time
"We are monitoring the market access very closely."
To adjust -- to change something a little to make it better
"Access is slow, so we are adjusting the plan this week."
To handle -- to deal with a task or a problem
"My team is handling the launch in the South region."
Situation -- what is happening at a certain moment
"The situation on the ground is changing fast."
On the ground -- in the real place where the work is happening
"Our reps are on the ground, visiting doctors every day."
In real time -- happening now, with no delay
"I am giving you the numbers in real time."
Status -- the current state of something
"Let me give you a quick status of the launch."
To run -- to manage or operate something that is active
"The team is running three campaigns at the moment."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

To monitor
To adjust
To handle
Situation
On the ground
In real time
Status
To run

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

David, from the Global team, calls Emmanuele for a quick status of the Brazilian launch. "Right now, my team is running the launch in three regions," she says. Normally, her reps visit doctors every week — that is the routine. But this week, they are visiting more than twenty doctors a day, and the team is seeing good growth in the South. There is one problem: in the Southeast, access is slow. "We are handling it, so we are adjusting the plan this week," she explains. David asks about the numbers. "We are monitoring everything in real time," she answers. "Our reps are on the ground every day, and I am tracking the results on one dashboard." David is happy: this is exactly the update the VP wants to hear.

1. "This week, they are visiting more than twenty doctors a day." Why the -ing form here?
A Because it is a plan for next year.
B Because it is happening NOW, this week. "This week" is the signal.
C Because "they" is plural.
2. "Normally, her reps visit doctors every week." Why "visit" and not "are visiting"?
A Because it is the routine — always true. "Every week" is the signal for present simple.
B Because it happened last week.
C Because "visit" is an irregular verb.
3. "My team is running the launch." Why "is" and not "are"?
A Because "team" is a past word.
B Because "team" is singular in American English: my team is.
C Because there is only one launch.
4. Which sentence shows Emmanuele is in control of the numbers, right now?
A "We visited the doctors last month."
B "We are monitoring everything in real time."
C "We are going to check the numbers next year."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Routine vs Right Now

GRAMMAR

A consolidation of the -ing form from lessons 4 and 6. Two times, one update: what you ALWAYS do, and what is happening NOW.

TimeUse it forExample
Present simple
(we visit)		Your routine and facts that are always true. Signals: every day, every week.We visit doctors every week.
Present continuous
(we are visiting)		What is happening NOW, this period. Signals: right now, at the moment, this week.This week, we are visiting doctors in São Paulo.
The formam / is / are + verb + -ing. The -ing never changes; only am/is/are changes.I am monitoring · the team is running · we are adjusting

Careful: "team" is singular in American English — "my team is running", not "my team are running". And with "right now" or "at the moment", always use the -ing form, never the simple.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Let me give you a quick of the launch."
"Right now, my team the launch."
"Every week, we doctors."
"Access is slow, so we the plan."
"We are monitoring the results ."
"Our reps are , visiting doctors."

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Put the lines of a live update in the correct order, from the opening status to the results.

?Hi David, thanks for the call. Let me give you a quick status.
?At the moment, we are seeing good growth in the South.
?We are monitoring the results in real time.
?Right now, my team is running the launch in three regions.
?We are handling one challenge, so we are adjusting the plan this week.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These are the five lines that carry a whole live update. Careful with MO-ni-tor — the strong part is the first one.

Let me give you a quick status of the launch.
Right now, my team is running the launch in three regions.
Our reps are on the ground, visiting doctors every day.
We are handling a challenge, so we are adjusting the plan.
We are monitoring the results in real time.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of a live update with the Global team.

David asks: "Give me a quick status of the launch." You say:
A "The launch was last month."
B "Right now, my team is running the launch in three regions."
C "We are going to run the launch next year."
You want to describe your routine (what you always do). You say:
A "Right now, we are visit doctors every week."
B "We visit doctors every week."
C "We are visiting doctors every week."
There is a problem with access, and you are working on it. You say:
A "We are handling it, so we are adjusting the plan this week."
B "We handle it last week."
C "We are going to handle it maybe."
The VP wants to know you are in control of the numbers, now. You say:
A "I check the numbers next month."
B "We are monitoring the results in real time."
C "The numbers are fine, I think."
You want to say where your reps are working. You say:
A "Our reps are in the office all day."
B "Our reps are on the ground, visiting doctors every day."
C "Our reps visited doctors last year."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

The Global team is on a call and asks: "What is your team doing right now in Brazil?" Give a full live update, out loud: open with a status, say what your team is doing right now (present continuous), describe the field ("our reps are on the ground..."), name one challenge you are handling and how you are adjusting the plan, and end by showing you are monitoring the results in real time. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1Let me give you a quick status of the launch.
2Right now, my team is running the launch.
3Our reps are on the ground, visiting doctors.
4We are handling a challenge, so we are adjusting the plan.
5We are monitoring the results in real time.
Lesson 12 -- Pre-class

Plans in Motion -- Talking About What's Coming Next

Present the plan already decided for the next quarter -- the kickoff, the launch, the rollout and the forecast -- with one pattern: going to + verb.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the words of a business plan.

Forecast -- a statement about future numbers, based on today's data
"The forecast for new patients is strong this quarter."
Initiative -- a new plan or project to reach a goal
"The team is going to start a new atopic dermatitis initiative."
Timeline -- a plan that shows when each step is going to happen
"The timeline is on the wall for everyone to see."
Phase -- one stage of a bigger plan
"The rollout is going to happen in three phases."
Roadmap -- a simple plan of the steps to reach a goal
"The Rain Plan is a simple roadmap for the third quarter."
Kickoff -- the official start of a project or a plan
"In July, the team is going to start the kickoff of the new initiative."
Budget -- the money a team plans to spend on a project
"The budget is ready, and the plan is approved."
To expand -- to make something bigger, or grow into new areas
"The team is going to expand the program to two new regions."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word to its meaning in English.

Forecast
Initiative
Timeline
Phase
Roadmap
Kickoff
Budget
To expand

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the short update, then answer the questions. Notice the words in bold.

Here is the plan for the third quarter. In July, we are going to start the kickoff of the new initiative. In August, we are going to launch the higher dose — that is the biggest milestone. The rollout is going to happen in three phases, and the team is going to expand the program to two new regions. The forecast for new patients is strong, and I am going to present all of this to Paris in ninety days.
1. Why does the text say "we are going to launch" and not "we is going to launch"?
A Because the subject is "we" (many), so it takes are. The am/is/are follows the subject.
B Because "launch" is a long word.
C Because every plan sentence uses "are".
2. The rollout "is going to happen". Why is the verb "happen" and not "happens"?
A Because "the rollout" is plural.
B Because the verb after "going to" always stays in its base form — happen, launch, start — never with -s.
C Because "happen" is in the past.
3. What does "going to" tell us about the plan in this update?
A That the plan already happened last quarter.
B That it is a plan already decided for the future — the kickoff, the launch and the rollout are coming.
C That the team is doing all of it right now, at this moment.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- One pattern for the whole plan: going to + base verb

GRAMMAR

Use going to for a plan that is already decided. The pattern is always the same: subject + am / is / are + going to + base verb. Only the am/is/are changes, following the subject. The verb after "going to" never changes.

Subjectam / is / are + going toExample
Iam going to + verbI am going to present the plan.
He / she / it / the rollout (one)is going to + verbThe rollout is going to happen in phases.
We / you / they (many)are going to + verbWe are going to launch the higher dose.
The rule to keepThe verb after going to is always the base form: going to launch, going to happen, going to expand. Never "going to launches", never "going to launched", and never drop the "to".

Common mistake: "we is going to launch" and "going to launches" — both are wrong. Quick fix: first choose am/is/are for the subject (we are, the rollout is), then keep the verb in its base form (launch, not launches).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct form of "going to". Before you write, ask: is the subject one or many? Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"In July, we start the kickoff."
"The rollout happen in three phases."
"I present the plan to Paris."
"The team is going to the program to two new regions."
"In August, they launch the higher dose."
"The forecast guide the budget."

Stage 2: Put the Plan in Order

Order

Put the plan in the correct order, from the opening to the closing. Look at the professional pattern: open, the first step, the milestone, the reach, the forecast.

?In August, we are going to launch the higher dose -- that is the biggest milestone.
?The forecast for new patients is strong, and I am going to present all of this to Paris.
?Good morning, Marc. Here is the plan for Brazil for the third quarter.
?The rollout is going to happen in three phases, and we are going to expand to two new regions.
?In July, we are going to start the kickoff of the new initiative.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen, then record yourself. Focus on the "going to" -- in natural speech it sounds like "gonna", but keep the full form clear.

In July, we are going to start the kickoff of the new initiative.
In August, we are going to launch the higher dose.
The rollout is going to happen in three phases.
We are going to expand the program to two new regions.
I am going to present the plan to Paris in ninety days.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz
Marc asks: "What is the team going to do in July?" You answer:
A "We is going to start the kickoff."
B "We are going to start the kickoff of the new initiative."
C "We are going to started the kickoff."
He asks when the launch is going to happen. You say:
A "We are going to launch the higher dose in August."
B "We are going to launches the higher dose in August."
C "We going launch the higher dose in August."
You want to describe the rollout, which is one thing. You say:
A "The rollout are going to happen in three phases."
B "The rollout is going to happen in three phases."
C "The rollout is going happen in three phases."
Marc asks the hard question: "What is going to happen if a target is not met?" You answer:
A "We are going to adjust the timeline and focus on two key regions."
B "I am very sorry, I do not know."
C "We are going to adjusting the timeline."
You close your update with one confident sentence. You say:
A "I am going to presented all of this to Paris."
B "I am going to present all of this to Paris in ninety days."
C "I going to present all of this to Paris."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

Marc Dubois asks you in the pre-presentation call: "Can you walk me through your team plan for the next quarter?" Present the plan for a REAL product of yours (atopic dermatitis, asthma or COPD), in this order: (1) open it -- "Here is the plan for..."; (2) the kickoff -- what you are going to start, and when; (3) the launch and the milestone -- what you are going to launch in which month; (4) the rollout -- how it is going to happen (in phases); (5) the reach and the forecast -- where you are going to expand, and the forecast for new patients; and (6) the hard question -- what you are going to do if a target is not met. Use at least four sentences with "going to". Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1In July, we are going to start the kickoff of the new initiative.
2In August, we are going to launch the higher dose.
3The rollout is going to happen in three phases.
4We are going to expand the program to two new regions.
5I am going to present the plan to Paris in ninety days.
Lesson 13 -- Pre-class

Bigger, Better, Stronger -- Comparing Products, Markets and Results

Compare Brazil for the Global team: how it compares to last year, which indication is the best, and where your advantage over the competitor is. Key words: competitor, benchmark, advantage, indication, portfolio, ranking, efficacy, to outperform. The grammar of the lesson is comparatives and superlatives — a consolidation of what you met in lesson 8. Compare two things with -er than or more ... than ("Brazil is stronger than last year", "atopic dermatitis is more important than DPOC") and pick the top of all with the -est or the most ("the biggest indication", "the most important market"). Careful with the irregulars: good → better → best, bad → worse → worst.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of a comparison.

Competitor -- another company that sells a similar product
"Our main competitor launched a new product this year."
Benchmark -- a standard number you use to compare results
"Last year is our benchmark for growth."
Advantage -- something that makes you better than others
"Fast access is a big advantage in this market."
Indication -- a health problem that a medicine treats
"Atopic dermatitis is our strongest indication."
Portfolio -- all the products a company sells
"Immunology is the biggest part of our portfolio."
Ranking -- a list that shows what is first, second and third
"Brazil moved up in the global ranking this quarter."
Efficacy -- how well a treatment works
"The new treatment shows higher efficacy than the old one."
To outperform -- to do better than someone or something
"This quarter, Brazil is outperforming the forecast."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Competitor
Benchmark
Advantage
Indication
Portfolio
Ranking
Efficacy
To outperform

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

David, from the Global team, asks Emmanuele how Brazil compares to last year. "Brazil is much stronger than last year," she says. "Our growth is higher, and access is faster." David wants to know which indication is performing the best. "Atopic dermatitis is our best indication," Emmanuele answers. "It is bigger than asthma in the private market." Then David asks about the competitor. "We have one clear advantage: higher efficacy," she explains. "That is why we are ahead of the curve." Finally, David asks if this is her most important market. "Yes. Brazil is one of the most important markets in our portfolio, and we are outperforming the forecast." David is happy: this is exactly the comparison the VP wants to hear.

1. "Brazil is stronger than last year." Why "stronger" and not "the strongest"?
A Because "strong" is an irregular word.
B Because she compares TWO things — this year and last year. Two things take -er + than.
C Because Brazil is the only market.
2. "Atopic dermatitis is our best indication." Why "best" and not "goodest"?
A Because "good" is irregular: good → better → the best. "Goodest" does not exist.
B Because "indication" is a long word.
C Because it happened in the past.
3. "Brazil is one of the most important markets." Why "most important" and not "importantest"?
A Because "important" happened last year.
B Because "important" is a long word — long words take "the most", not "-est".
C Because there are many markets.
4. Which sentence shows Brazil is doing better than expected?
A "We visited the doctors last month."
B "We are outperforming the forecast."
C "The competitor is bigger than us."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Comparatives and Superlatives

GRAMMAR

A consolidation of what you met in lesson 8. To compare TWO things, use the comparative. For the top of ALL, use the superlative.

AdjectiveComparative (two things)Superlative (top of all)
Short word
big, high, low, strong		+ -er than
bigger, higher, stronger		the + -est
the biggest, the highest
Long word
important, complex		more ... than
more important		the most ...
the most important
Irregular
good, bad		better / worse thanthe best / the worst

Careful: never use two forms together — "more bigger" does not exist. It is "bigger" (compare two) or "the biggest" (top of all). And "good" is irregular: better, the best — never "gooder" or "goodest".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Brazil is than last year."
"Atopic dermatitis is our indication."
"Our growth is than the forecast."
"Atopic dermatitis is important than asthma."
"Brazil is one of the important markets in our portfolio."
"We have one clear : higher efficacy."

Stage 2: Put the Comparison in Order

Order

Put the lines of a comparison in the correct order, from the opening to the results.

?Hi David, let me compare the numbers for you.
?Our growth is higher, and access is faster.
?And we are outperforming the forecast this quarter.
?Compared to last year, Brazil is much stronger.
?Atopic dermatitis is our best indication.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These are the five lines that carry a whole comparison. Careful with com-PE-ti-tor — the strong part is the second one.

Compared to last year, Brazil is much stronger.
Our growth is higher, and access is faster.
Atopic dermatitis is our best indication.
We have a clear advantage: higher efficacy.
Brazil is one of the most important markets in our portfolio.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of a comparison with the Global team.

David asks: "How does Brazil compare to last year?" You say:
A "Brazil is more bigger than last year."
B "Compared to last year, Brazil is much stronger."
C "Brazil is the most strong than last year."
You want to say which indication is number one of all. You say:
A "Atopic dermatitis is a more good indication."
B "Atopic dermatitis is our best indication."
C "Atopic dermatitis is the goodest indication."
You compare two indications (atopic dermatitis and asthma). You say:
A "Atopic dermatitis is bigger than asthma in the private market."
B "Atopic dermatitis is the bigger than asthma."
C "Atopic dermatitis is more big than asthma."
The VP asks about the competitor. You show your advantage. You say:
A "The competitor is the more important."
B "We have a clear advantage: higher efficacy."
C "Our efficacy is gooder than the competitor."
You want to place Brazil at the top of the portfolio. You say:
A "Brazil is one of the importantest markets."
B "Brazil is one of the most important markets in our portfolio."
C "Brazil is one of the more important markets in our portfolio."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

The Global team is on a call and asks: "How does Brazil compare this year?" Give a full comparison, out loud: open with "Compared to last year...", say what is stronger or higher, name your best indication and how it compares to another (present continuous is fine), show your advantage over the competitor (higher efficacy), and end by placing Brazil among the most important markets in the portfolio. Use at least two comparatives and one superlative. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1Compared to last year, Brazil is much stronger.
2Our growth is higher, and access is faster.
3Atopic dermatitis is our best indication.
4Brazil is one of the most important markets for us.
5We are ahead of the curve on efficacy.
Lesson 14 -- Pre-class

Checking In and Speaking Up -- Asking for Clarification in Global Meetings

The class that ends the freezing on a Global call. Three small toolkits keep you in the room: clarify ("Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that?", "What do you mean by...?"), hold the floor ("Let me finish, please.") and buy time ("That's a good question. Let me think for a second."). Key words: clarify, concern, wrap up, misunderstanding, interrupt, raise, feedback, summarize. This is functional language — no new grammar — with present continuous (we are changing the plan) and going to (I'm going to follow up) in review. Idiom of the lesson: "Let's take that offline."
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of a meeting — what you do, and what you ask for on a Global call.

Clarify -- to explain something again so it is clear
"Could you clarify that last number for me?"
Concern -- a worry or a problem you want to talk about
"I have one concern about the timeline."
Wrap up -- to finish a meeting or a topic
"Let's wrap up and agree on the next steps."
Misunderstanding -- when two people understand the same thing in different ways
"Sorry, that was a small misunderstanding."
Interrupt -- to stop someone while they are speaking
"Sorry to interrupt, but I have a quick question."
Raise -- to bring up a point or a question in a meeting
"I would like to raise one point about the budget."
Feedback -- comments about your work, to help you improve
"Thank you for the feedback on my report."
Summarize -- to say the main points again, in a short way
"Let me summarize the three action points."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Clarify
Concern
Wrap up
Misunderstanding
Interrupt
Raise
Feedback
Summarize

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It is a Global call, and Marc is talking fast. "We are changing the launch plan, and I am going to send the new figures after the call." Emmanuele misses the last part. The old Emmanuele would stay quiet and understand it later. The new one speaks up: "Sorry, Marc, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?"

Marc repeats it, and she checks the word: "What do you mean by 'the new figures' — the sales, or the targets?" Then he asks a hard question, and she does not freeze. She buys time: "That's a good question. Let me think for a second." She gives her opinion, and adds a concern about the timeline: "The public market is slower, so I am going to follow up with the access team this week."

Then someone interrupts her. The old Emmanuele would stop. The new one holds the floor: "Sorry, let me just finish my point, please." Nobody minds. In fact, the room respects it. She did not understand every word — but she stayed in the meeting from the first minute to the last.

1. Marc says, "We are changing the launch plan." Why this form?
A It is the present continuous — something that is happening now, around this moment.
B It is the past — the plan changed last year.
C It is a question about the future.
2. When Emmanuele misses the last part, what does she do?
A She stays quiet and tries to understand it later, after the call.
B She asks for clarification: "Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?"
C She says "What? Repeat!" in one word.
3. Marc asks a hard question. Before she answers, she says, "Let me think for a second." Why?
A To end the meeting.
B To buy time — two calm seconds to think, instead of freezing.
C To ask Marc to repeat the question.
4. When she says, "I am going to follow up with the access team," she is talking about:
A Something she did yesterday.
B A plan for the future — "going to" for what she will do.
C Something that is happening at this exact second.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Three boxes, and the tenses you already know

GRAMMAR

There is no new grammar today — only three ready toolkits, plus two tenses you already have. In a meeting you use the present continuous for what is happening now ("we are changing the plan") and going to for what you will do ("I am going to follow up"). The toolkits are the phrases that keep you in the room when you miss a word.

The toolkitWhen you use itWhat you say
CLARIFYYou miss a word, or a point is not clear"Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that?" · "What do you mean by...?" · "Just to clarify..."
HOLD THE FLOORSomeone interrupts you"Let me finish, please." · "Can I just add one thing?"
BUY TIMEYou don't know the answer yet"That's a good question. Let me think for a second."
The one ruleLearn one phrase from each box by heart. Add the soft words — sorry, please, could, just — and you always sound professional, never abrupt.

Common mistake: under pressure, it is easy to cut the words and sound hard: "Repeat!", "What?", "No, I talk!". The message is fine, but the tone sounds rude. The fix is not more grammar — it is the small words. "Repeat!" becomes "Sorry, could you repeat that, please?". "What?" becomes "Sorry, what do you mean by that?". Same idea, but now you sound like the calmest professional in the room.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line from a real meeting. Ask yourself first: which tool is this — clarify, hold the floor, or buy time? Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Sorry, I didn't that. Could you repeat that, please?"
"What do you by that, exactly?"
"Just to , you need the report by Friday?"
"Let me , please. I have one more point."
"That's a good question. Let me for a second."
"Can we take that and follow up later?"

Stage 2: Put the Exchange in Order

Order

Put this clarification exchange in the correct order, from the first reaction to the closing plan. Tap Listen to hear the whole exchange read aloud.

?Just to clarify, you need them by Friday?
?I have one concern, and I'm going to follow up this week.
?And what do you mean by the new figures?
?Let me think for a second before I answer.
?Sorry, Marc, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat the last part, please?

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five phrases that save any meeting. Keep them calm and warm: the soft words (sorry, please, could) do the work.

Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?
What do you mean by that, exactly?
Just to clarify, you need the report by Friday?
Let me finish, please. I have one more point.
That's a good question. Let me think for a second.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of a Global call with the Sanofi team.

Marc speaks fast and you miss a number. You say:
A "Repeat! I not understand."
B "Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat the last number, please?"
C "You talk very fast, I lost."
He uses a word you don't know: "churn". The best way to ask is:
A "What means 'churn'?"
B "What do you mean by 'churn', exactly?"
C "Churn? No idea, sorry."
Marc asks a hard question and you need two seconds to think. You say:
A "That's a good question. Let me think for a second."
B (silence, and then you freeze)
C "I don't know. Next question."
You are speaking and a colleague interrupts you. You want to keep your turn. You say:
A "No, no, is my turn, I talk!"
B (you stop and go quiet)
C "Sorry, let me just finish this point, please."
The pricing topic is too big for today's meeting. You say:
A "Let's go to the internet and see the price."
B "Can we take that offline and follow up next week?"
C "Pricing is not important now, forget it."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

You are on a Global call with the Sanofi team. It is moving fast. Imagine the meeting and speak out loud, using all three tools: (1) CLARIFY — you miss a word or a number, so ask for it again ("Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that?") and check a term ("What do you mean by...?"); (2) BUY TIME — someone asks you a hard question about the Brazil results, so take two seconds first ("That's a good question. Let me think for a second."); (3) HOLD THE FLOOR — someone interrupts you, so keep your turn ("Let me finish, please."). Add one real concern about your market, and one plan with "I'm going to...". Use at least four of the meeting phrases. Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1Sorry, I didn't catch that. Could you repeat that, please?
2What do you mean by that, exactly?
3Let me finish, please. I have one more point.
4That's a good question. Let me think for a second.
5Can we take that offline and follow up later?
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

Have You Replied Yet? -- Handling the Global Inbox on a Follow-up Call

Answer your inbox out loud for David and the Global team: what you have already done, what you have just done, and what you haven't finished yet. Key words: inbox, reply, attachment, reminder, draft, thread, to forward, to reach out. The grammar of the lesson is the present perfect with just, already and yet. Say an action is done with have / has + the verb ("I have sent the report"). Use just for a moment ago ("I've just replied"), already for done sooner than expected ("I've already forwarded it"), and yet for what is still open, in questions and negatives ("Have you sent it yet?", "Not yet").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of your inbox and your follow-up call.

Inbox -- where your new emails arrive
"I have twenty emails in my inbox this morning."
Reply -- the answer you send back to an email
"I sent a quick reply to the London team."
Attachment -- a file you send together with an email
"The report is in the attachment."
Reminder -- a message that helps you not forget something
"I set a reminder to reply before Friday."
Draft -- an email you started but have not sent yet
"The summary is still a draft in my inbox."
Thread -- all the emails on the same topic together
"Please read the whole thread before you reply."
To forward -- to send an email you received to another person
"I'll forward the thread to the Paris team."
To reach out -- to contact someone to ask or to help
"I'll reach out to the market access team today."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Inbox
Reply
Attachment
Reminder
Draft
Thread
To forward
To reach out

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

David, from the Global team, calls Emmanuele for a quick follow-up. "Have you sent the report to London?" he asks. "Yes, I have already sent it," she says. "And I have forwarded the thread to you too." David asks about Claire's email. "I have just replied to her — I answered her this morning," Emmanuele says. Then David asks about the access summary. "I haven't finished it yet. It is still a draft in my inbox." David asks her to reach out to the market access team. "Sure. I have already set a reminder," she answers. David is happy: Emmanuele knows exactly what is done and what is still open.

1. "I have already sent it." What does this mean?
A She is going to send it tomorrow.
B The action is done — and done sooner than expected.
C She never sends it.
2. "I have just replied to her." Why "just"?
A Because she replied a very short time ago — a moment ago.
B Because she replied last year.
C Because she will reply next week.
3. "I haven't finished it yet." What does "yet" tell us?
A The summary is finished.
B The summary is not finished — it is still open.
C The summary does not exist.
4. Which sentence is a question about what is still open?
A "I sent the report yesterday."
B "Have you sent the report yet?"
C "I am going to send the report."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect: just / already / yet

GRAMMAR

To say an action is done — without saying exactly when — use have / has + the verb. Three small words tell the story.

FormStructureExample
Positive (+)
done		have + just / already + verbI have just sent it.
I have already replied.
Negative (−)
still open		haven't + verb + yetI haven't finished it yet.
Question (?)Have you + verb + yet?Have you sent it yet?

Careful: use the verb in the right form — "have sent", not "have send". Put just and already between have and the verb. Put yet at the end, and only in questions and negatives — in a positive sentence, use already, not "yet".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I have the report to London."
"I have replied to her email."
"I have forwarded the thread to you."
"I haven't finished the summary ."
" you replied to Claire yet?"
"The summary is still a in my inbox."

Stage 2: Put the Update in Order

Order

Put the lines of an inbox update in the correct order, from the opening to what is still open.

?Hi David, let me give you a quick update.
?I've just replied to Claire's email.
?And I've already set a reminder to reach out to the team.
?I've already sent the report to London.
?I haven't finished the access summary yet.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These are the five lines that carry a whole follow-up. Careful with a-TACH-ment — the strong part is the second one.

I've already sent the report to London.
I've just replied to Claire's email.
I've forwarded the thread to the Paris team.
I haven't finished the access summary yet.
Have you replied to Claire yet?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of a follow-up call with the Global team.

David asks: "Have you sent the report to London?" It is done. You say:
A "I have send it already."
B "Yes, I've already sent it."
C "I have sent it yet."
You replied to Claire a moment ago. You say:
A "I have reply just to her."
B "I've just replied to her."
C "I did just reply to her yet."
The access summary is not ready. You say:
A "I haven't finished it yet. It's still a draft."
B "I have finished it yet."
C "I don't finished it already."
You want to ask David if he has replied to the London thread. You say:
A "Did you reply to the thread yet?"
B "Have you replied to the thread yet?"
C "Have you reply to the thread already yet?"
You already set a reminder to contact the access team. You say:
A "I have already set a reminder to reach out yet."
B "I've already set a reminder to reach out to them."
C "I set already a reminder for reach out."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

David calls for a follow-up and asks: "What have you done today, and what is still open?" Give a full inbox update, out loud: open with "Hi David, quick update...", say two things you have already done or just done (already sent, just replied, forwarded the thread), say one thing you haven't finished yet (the access summary), and end with what you will do next (reach out to the team this afternoon). Use at least two positive sentences with already or just, and one negative with yet. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1I've already sent it.
2I've just replied to her email.
3I haven't finished it yet.
4Have you sent it yet?
5Let me get back to you on that.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

What Comes Next? -- Forecasting Brazil's Results for the Global Team

After you report the past, the Global team always asks for one more thing: the forecast. This is the lesson of will — the language of prediction. You use it to say what you expect, what you predict, and what you think will happen with the Brazil numbers: "Sales will increase", "It will stay steady", "We will have a small shortfall, but it will recover". Key words: projection, estimate, increase, decrease, predict, steady, peak, shortfall. New grammar: will for predictions and forecasts (with going to, the decided plan, in contrast).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of a forecast — the numbers going up, going down, and the honest ones in the middle.

Projection -- a number you expect in the future, based on your data
"My projection for Q4 is a strong increase."
Estimate -- an approximate number, not the exact one
"This is only an estimate, not the final number."
Increase -- when a number goes up
"We expect an increase in the second half."
Decrease -- when a number goes down
"There was a small decrease last month."
Predict -- to say what you think will happen
"I predict a strong year for immunology."
Steady -- slow and regular, without big changes
"After the peak, the numbers stay steady."
Peak -- the highest point
"Sales reached their peak in September."
Shortfall -- when you have less than the plan
"We have a small shortfall in the public market."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Projection
Estimate
Increase
Decrease
Predict
Steady
Peak
Shortfall

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

The Global team has the Q3 results. Now Marc asks the question they always ask: "What do you predict for Q4?" Emmanuele is ready. "My projection is positive. I think sales will increase by about eight percent." Marc wants more: "Will that be steady?" She answers with the same small word: "Yes, after the peak in September, the numbers will stay steady."

Then the honest part. The public market is below plan. She does not hide it: "We will probably have a small shortfall there — about five percent. But it will recover in Q1." Notice the word in every prediction: will. She uses will for what she expects from the numbers — not for a plan already decided (that is "going to": "We are going to launch in Q3"), but for what she thinks will happen.

Marc is happy. "An honest forecast is more useful than a perfect one." And that is the whole idea: the past results are the story, but the forecast — what will happen next — is the value. A manager who can say "here is where we are, and here is where I think we will be" is a manager the Global team trusts.

1. Emmanuele says, "Sales will increase by about eight percent." Why this form?
A It is a prediction about the future — "will" + the simple verb.
B It is the past — sales increased last year.
C It is something happening at this exact second.
2. The public market is below plan. What does Emmanuele do?
A She hides the number so the team does not worry.
B She reports the shortfall honestly, then predicts the recovery: "It will recover in Q1."
C She says the market is perfect.
3. What is the difference between "will" and "going to" in the text?
A They mean exactly the same thing.
B "going to" = a plan already decided; "will" = what she predicts from the numbers.
C "will" is for the past and "going to" is for now.
4. According to the text, why is the forecast so important?
A Because only the past results matter to the Global team.
B The past is the story, but the forecast — what will happen next — is the value the team trusts.
C Because a forecast must always be a perfect, exact number.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- "will" for predictions

GRAMMAR

This is the easiest grammar in English: will never changes, and the verb after it stays simple. You use will to say what you predict and expect about the future. Keep going to for the plan you already decided, and use will for what the numbers point to.

UseStructureExample
A prediction (+)will + base verb (same for I / you / it)"Sales will increase." · "It will stay steady."
Negative (−)will not (won't) + base verb"We won't miss the target by much."
Question (?)Will + subject + base verb?"Will Brazil reach the goal?"
will vs going togoing to = a plan already decided ("We are going to launch in Q3"). will = what you predict from the numbers ("I think it will be a success").

Common mistake: after will, use the simple verb — no to, no -s. Not "Sales will to increase" and not "It will grows", but "Sales will increase" and "It will grow". And for a prediction about the future, use will, never the present: not "Next quarter is better", but "Next quarter will be better".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line from a real forecast. Remember the rule: after "will", the simple verb. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"My for next quarter is positive."
"I think sales increase by about eight percent."
"After the peak, the numbers will stay ."
"We will probably have a small , but it will recover."
"This is only an , not the final number."
"I a strong year for immunology."

Stage 2: Put the Forecast in Order

Order

Put this forecast in the correct order, from the opening projection to the full-year estimate. Tap Listen to hear the whole forecast read aloud.

?After the peak in September, the numbers will stay steady.
?But it will recover in Q1, so the full-year estimate is strong.
?I predict sales will increase by about eight percent.
?The public market will have a small shortfall this quarter.
?My projection for Q4 is positive.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five phrases that build any forecast. Keep the small word clear: will.

My projection for next quarter is positive.
I think sales will increase by about eight percent.
After the peak, the numbers will stay steady.
We will probably have a small shortfall, but it will recover.
I predict this year will be stronger than last year.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of a forecast call with the Sanofi Global team.

Marc asks about next quarter's sales. You predict growth. You say:
A "Sales will to increase eight percent."
B "I think sales will increase by about eight percent."
C "Sales increase next quarter, sure."
He asks if the growth will be regular. You say:
A "Yes, it will grows steady."
B "Yes, after the peak, it will stay steady."
C "Yes, is steady, no problem."
The public market is below plan. The honest, professional answer is:
A "Everything is perfect there, no problem."
B "We will probably have a small shortfall, but it will recover in Q1."
C "I don't want to talk about that market."
You only have an approximate number, not the exact one. You say:
A "This is only an estimate, but I predict around five percent."
B "The number is exactly five percent, for sure."
C "I don't know any number."
The launch date is already decided for Q3. Here you use:
A "We will launch, maybe, I predict." (a guess)
B "We are going to launch in Q3." (a decided plan)
C "We launch in Q3 yesterday."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

You are opening a forecast for the Global team. Imagine the Brazil dashboard and speak out loud, using "will" at least five times: (1) give your PROJECTION for the private market ("I think sales will increase by about eight percent"); (2) say the growth WILL be steady after the peak; (3) report the public market SHORTFALL honestly, then predict the recovery ("We will probably have a small shortfall, but it will recover in Q1"); (4) close with your ESTIMATE for the full year ("My estimate is that we will finish on target"). Use projection, estimate, increase, predict, steady, peak and shortfall. Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1My projection for next quarter is positive.
2I think sales will increase by about eight percent.
3After the peak, the numbers will stay steady.
4We will probably have a small shortfall, but it will recover.
5This is only an estimate, but it is my best one.
Lesson 17 -- Pre-class

Midpoint Milestone -- Progress Review: Looking Back at 16 Weeks

The halfway mark. This is a review: no new grammar, no new words. You look back at the first half and get ready to describe your role at Sanofi out loud. Review the consolidated words: results, challenge, launch, target, performance, benchmark, growth, gap. Review the five structures you already use — the past ("I presented"), the present ("I manage"), what is happening now ("I'm working on..."), your plans ("I'm going to..."), and comparatives ("stronger than last year"). The one new expression is "Looking back at...", to talk about your progress.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words you already own from the first half — review, not new.

Results -- the numbers your work produces
"I present the Brazil results to the Global team."
Challenge -- a difficult thing you must handle
"Our biggest challenge this year is market access."
Launch -- when a new product first goes on the market
"The launch is planned for the second half."
Target -- the number or goal you want to reach
"We reached our target for the first half."
Performance -- how well a team or product is doing
"The team's performance was strong this quarter."
Benchmark -- a standard you compare your results against
"Brazil is above the global benchmark this year."
Growth -- when a number goes up over time
"Our growth this year is stronger than last year."
Gap -- the space between where you are and your target
"There is a small gap between our target and our result."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Results
Challenge
Launch
Target
Performance
Benchmark
Growth
Gap

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions. Notice the five structures you already use.

It is the midpoint review. David asks Emmanuele about her first half. Looking back at January, she remembers she translated every sentence in her head. Now, she says, "I present the Brazil results in English, and right now I am working on the Rain Plan and the launch." David asks about the numbers. "Our growth this year is stronger than last year, and we are above the benchmark," she answers. David asks about the second half. "My biggest challenge is speed. In August, I am going to present to the Global team." David is impressed: Emmanuele is not the same speaker she was in January.

1. "Looking back at January, she translated every sentence." Which moment is this?
A A plan for the future.
B The past — a finished action.
C Something happening right now.
2. "Right now I am working on the Rain Plan." Why "am working"?
A Because it is happening now, in progress.
B Because it happened last year.
C Because it is a routine every day.
3. "Our growth is stronger than last year." What is this?
A A plan for August.
B A comparison between this year and last year.
C A finished action in the past.
4. "In August, I am going to present to the team." What does this show?
A A finished action.
B A plan for the future.
C A comparison.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Five Structures You Carry (Review)

GRAMMAR

No new grammar today — just the five structures you have used all half, side by side. Each one is for a different moment.

MomentStructureExample
The pastverb + -ed (or irregular)"Last quarter, I presented the results."
The present (routine)verb (base form)"I manage eight disease areas."
Happening nowam / is / are + verb-ing"Right now, I am working on the launch."
A planam / is / are + going to + verb"In August, I am going to present."
Comparingadjective + -er + than"This year is stronger than last year."

To talk about your progress, open with the new expression "Looking back at...": "Looking back at the first half, I'm proud of my progress." Careful: keep the -ing for now ("am working", not "am work"), and use -er + than for short adjectives ("stronger than", not "more strong than").

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Looking back at the first half, I'm of my progress."
"Last quarter, I the results to David."
"Right now, I am on the Rain Plan."
"This year is than last year."
"In August, I am to present to the Global team."
"My biggest is speed."

Stage 2: Put the Recap in Order

Order

Put the lines of a midpoint recap in the correct order, from the opening to the plan.

?Looking back at the first half, I am proud of my progress.
?Now I present the Brazil results in English.
?My biggest challenge is speed, and I am going to work on it.
?In January, I translated every sentence in my head.
?Our growth this year is stronger than last year.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence and then record yourself saying it. These five lines cover a whole midpoint recap — one for each structure.

Looking back at the first half, I'm proud of my progress.
Last quarter, I presented the results to David.
Right now, I'm working on the Rain Plan.
This year is stronger than last year.
In August, I'm going to present to the Global team.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each moment of your midpoint review with the Global team.

David asks: "What did you do in the first half?" You say:
A "I present the results to the team."
B "I presented the results to the team."
C "I am going to present the results."
The VP asks what you are doing at this moment. You say:
A "Right now, I'm working on the Rain Plan."
B "Right now, I work on the Rain Plan yesterday."
C "Right now, I am work on the Rain Plan."
You want to compare this year to last year. You say:
A "This year is more strong than last year."
B "This year is stronger than last year."
C "This year is strong than last year."
David asks about your plan for August. You say:
A "In August, I present to the Global team."
B "In August, I'm going to present to the Global team."
C "In August, I presented to the Global team."
You want to open your progress recap. You say:
A "Looking back at the first half, I'm proud of my progress."
B "Look back the first half, I proud my progress."
C "Looking back at the first half, I going to proud."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two or three minutes, with no script.

This is your midpoint speaking task. Describe your role at Sanofi and what you are working on now. Open with "Looking back at the first half...", say what you did (past), what you manage every day (present), what you are working on right now (I'm working on...), how this year compares to last year (stronger than...), and your plan for August (I'm going to...). Use all five structures. Take your time and do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 17

1Looking back at the first half, I'm proud of my progress.
2Our growth is stronger than last year.
3Right now, I'm working on the launch.
4My biggest challenge is speed.
5In August, I'm going to present to the Global team.
Lesson 18 -- Pre-class

The Rain Plan -- Presenting Upcoming Launches and the Backup Plan to Global

The Global team never wants only the plan — they want the plan behind the plan. This is the lesson of the first conditional: "If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it." You use it to present your backup plan — what you will do if the numbers change, if you are behind schedule, or if it rains on launch day. Key words: backup plan, setback, delay, postpone, reschedule, in advance, ahead of schedule, behind schedule. New grammar: the first conditional (if + present simple, will + base verb), next to going to, the plan you already decided.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. These are the eight words of a plan that can change — the delays, the backups, and the schedule.

Backup plan -- a second plan you use if the first plan does not work
"If the launch is delayed, we start the backup plan."
Setback -- a problem that stops progress for a while
"A short delay is a small setback, not a disaster."
Delay -- extra time before something happens; when something is late
"There is a small delay in the product approval."
Postpone -- to move something to a later date
"If approval is late, we will postpone the launch."
Reschedule -- to give something a new date or time
"We will reschedule the congress for November."
In advance -- before the time something happens; early, with time to spare
"Send the slide in advance of the meeting."
Ahead of schedule -- earlier than the planned time
"If demand is high, we will launch ahead of schedule."
Behind schedule -- later than the planned time
"If we are behind schedule, we will use the backup plan."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word with its definition.

Backup plan
Setback
Delay
Postpone
Reschedule
In advance
Ahead of schedule
Behind schedule

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Emmanuele presents the upcoming launches to the Global team. The normal plan is clear: "We are going to launch the new product in Q3, on schedule." But Marc asks the hard question the board always asks: "And what is your backup plan if something changes?"

Emmanuele is ready. She has a Rain Plan. "If the product approval is late, we will postpone the launch by one month. If we are behind schedule, we will start with the private market first, and we will reschedule the congress for November. And if it rains on launch day, we will move the event online." Notice the shape of every sentence: if + the present (is, are, rains), then will + the simple verb (will postpone, will start, will move).

This is not being negative — it is the opposite. A manager who has a backup plan for every setback is a manager the Global team trusts. A delay is not a disaster if you already know your next move. Marc is happy: "A plan with a backup plan. I like this." The normal plan shows what you hope; the Rain Plan shows that you are ready.

1. Emmanuele says, "If the product approval is late, we will postpone the launch." Why this form?
A First conditional: after "if" the present (is), then "will" + the simple verb (will postpone).
B It is the past — the approval was late last year.
C Both verbs should use "will": "If the approval will be late, we will postpone."
2. What does the Global team want, besides the normal plan?
A Nothing — the normal plan is enough for them.
B The backup plan: what she will do if something changes.
C A promise that nothing will ever change.
3. In "If it rains on launch day, we will move online", where does "will" go?
A After "if": "If it will rain..."
B Only in the second half: "we will move" (after "if", the present: "it rains").
C In both halves of the sentence.
4. According to the text, why is a backup plan a good thing?
A Because it means the launch will definitely fail.
B A manager who is ready for every setback is a manager the Global team trusts.
C Because it lets you avoid ever presenting a normal plan.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- the first conditional

GRAMMAR

Use the first conditional for a real future condition — your backup plan. It has two halves: if + the present simple, then will + the base verb. Keep going to for the plan you already decided, and use if... will... for what you will do if things change.

UseStructureExample
A real future conditionIf + present simple, ... will + base verb"If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it."
Order can switch... will + base verb ... if + present"We will postpone it if the launch is delayed."
Negative (−)If + present, ... will not (won't) + base"If we are on track, we won't need the plan."
if... will... vs going togoing to = a plan already decided ("We are going to launch in Q3"). if... will... = the backup plan ("If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it").

Common mistake: after if, use the present — never will. Not "If the launch will be delayed", but "If the launch is delayed". And do not forget the will in the second half: not "If we are behind schedule, we postpone it", but "If we are behind schedule, we will postpone it".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each line from a real Rain Plan. Remember the rule: after "if", the present; "will" in the second half. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If the approval is late, we postpone the launch."
"If it on launch day, we will move the event online."
"If we behind schedule, we will use the backup plan."
"If the numbers are strong, we will the rollout."
"We will the congress for November."
"If we are on track, we need the backup plan."

Stage 2: Put the Rain Plan in Order

Order

Put this Rain Plan in the correct order, from the normal plan to the good-news scenario. Tap Listen to hear the whole plan read aloud.

?If we are behind schedule, we will start with the private market first.
?And if the numbers are strong, we will expand the rollout, ahead of schedule.
?But if the product approval is late, we will postpone the launch by one month.
?If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online.
?The normal plan is to launch in Q3, on schedule.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it. These are the five phrases that build any Rain Plan. Keep the two halves clear: if... will...

The normal plan is to launch in Q3, on schedule.
If the launch is delayed, we will use the backup plan.
If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online.
If we are behind schedule, we will postpone the congress.
If the numbers are strong, we will expand, ahead of schedule.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each real moment of a Rain Plan call with the Sanofi Global team.

Marc asks, "What will you do if the launch is delayed?" You say:
A "If the launch will be delayed, we will postpone it."
B "If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it by one month."
C "If the launch is delayed, we postpone it."
The event is outside and rain is possible. Your backup plan is:
A "If it will rain, we cancel everything."
B "If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online."
C "We move online, if rains maybe."
You are behind schedule in October. The professional answer is:
A "I don't have a plan for that."
B "If we are behind schedule, we will start with the private market first and reschedule the congress."
C "Everything is fine, no problem, forget it."
You want to send the Rain Plan slide early, before the meeting. You say:
A "I will send the slide in advance of the meeting."
B "I will send the slide behind schedule."
C "I send the slide after the meeting."
The launch date is already decided for Q3. Here you use:
A "If we launch, maybe in Q3, I think." (a guess)
B "We are going to launch in Q3." (a decided plan)
C "We launched in Q3 next month."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the situation below. There is no right or wrong answer — speak for two minutes, with no script.

You are presenting the Brazil Rain Plan to the Global team. Imagine your Q3 launch and speak out loud, using "if... will..." at least five times: (1) give the NORMAL plan with going to ("We are going to launch in Q3, on schedule"); (2) the backup if the approval is late ("If the approval is late, we will postpone the launch"); (3) the backup if you are behind schedule ("If we are behind schedule, we will start with the private market and reschedule the congress"); (4) the backup for bad weather ("If it rains, we will move the event online"); (5) the good-news scenario ("If the numbers are strong, we will expand ahead of schedule"). Use backup plan, postpone, reschedule, in advance, behind schedule and ahead of schedule. Speak for two minutes. Do not read from a script.

Survival Card -- Lesson 18

1The normal plan is to launch in Q3, on schedule.
2If the launch is delayed, we will use the backup plan.
3If it rains on launch day, we will move the event online.
4If we are behind schedule, we will postpone the congress.
5If the numbers are strong, we will expand, ahead of schedule.

IN CLASS -- Select your Lesson

01
Diagnostic + First Words -- Who Is Emmanuele Orrico?
Introducing yourself and your role to the Sanofi Global team -- 33 slides
02
Your Day and Your Work Routine -- A Week at Sanofi Brazil
Describing your typical week to the Global team: schedule, field visits, reports -- 35 slides
03
What Happened Last Quarter? -- Reporting Past Results
Telling the Global team what happened: targets, challenges and opportunities of the last quarter -- 33 slides
04
Breaking the Ice -- Small Talk at a Global Event
The dinner before the presentation: introducing yourself, saying what you are working on right now, and keeping the conversation alive -- 35 slides
05
What Are Your Plans? -- Launches, Meetings and Goals
O que vem pela frente: o plano do trimestre, a agenda de agosto e a apresentação para o time Global -- 34 slides
06
How Is It Going? -- Reporting Progress to the Global Team
O check-in de meio de caminho: quantos centros estão on track, quanto progresso há de verdade, e o que ainda falta antes de agosto -- 35 slides
07
What's Next? -- Next Steps and Offers in the Global Meeting
O fim da reunião: resumir os próximos passos que você já planejou e fazer ofertas na hora -- "I'll send you the report" -- sem travar -- 34 slides
08
Better, Bigger, Best -- Comparing Results in the Business Review
A hora da apresentação: comparar o Brasil com a região, uma área com a outra, este ano com o passado -- "Brazil is bigger than Mexico, but the growth is higher" -- sem travar -- 35 slides
09
Networking in English -- Starting and Keeping a Conversation
Como abrir, manter e fechar uma conversa em inglês num evento global: o coquetel, o jantar e o momento de trocar contatos -- 35 slides
10
How Much and How Many? -- Talking About Patients, Data and Market Access
Fechando o bloco 6-10: como falar dos números que importam -- quantos pacientes, quanta evidência, quanto acesso -- e como o acesso mudou (o que used to ser e o que é hoje) -- 35 slides
11
What's Happening Right Now -- Describing Live Situations
Dar um status ao vivo para o time Global: o que a sua equipe está fazendo AGORA no Brasil -- o lançamento, o campo e os resultados em tempo real, com o present continuous -- 34 slides
12
Plans in Motion -- Talking About What's Coming Next
Reading model: the Rain Plan for Q3 -- how to present a plan already decided (going to), the kickoff, the milestones, the rollout and the forecast for the Global team -- 35 slides
13
Bigger, Better, Stronger -- Comparing Products, Markets and Results
Comparar para o time Global: como o Brasil se compara com o ano passado, qual indicação vai melhor e onde está a vantagem sobre o concorrente -- consolidação de comparativos e superlativos -- 34 slides
14
Checking In and Speaking Up -- Asking for Clarification in Global Meetings
O remédio para o travamento na call: as três caixas de ferramentas -- pedir para esclarecer, segurar a palavra e ganhar tempo -- para nunca mais ficar em silêncio numa reunião com a Global -- 37 slides
15
