Have You Replied Yet? -- Handling the Global Inbox on a Follow-up Call
Responder a fila de emails do time Global em voz alta, numa ligação de follow-up: dizer o que você JA fez, o que ACABOU de fazer e o que AINDA NAO fez -- present perfect com just, already e yet -- 34 slides
16
What Comes Next? -- Forecasting Brazil's Results for the Global Team
Depois de reportar o passado, o passo que a Global sempre pede a seguir: a PREVISÃO. A aula do 'will' -- a linguagem do forecast -- para dizer o que você espera, prevê e projeta dos números do Brasil, com honestidade até no shortfall -- 37 slides
17
Midpoint Milestone -- Progress Review: Looking Back at 16 Weeks
Meio do caminho: revisar o que ela ja domina e ela mesma descrever, em voz alta, seu papel na Sanofi e seus resultados. Reune tudo -- past simple, present simple, present continuous, going to e comparativos -- e o vocabulario consolidado (results, challenge, launch, target, benchmark, growth, gap). Nova expressao: 'Looking back at...' -- 32 slides
18
The Rain Plan -- Presenting Upcoming Launches and the Backup Plan to Global
A Global não quer só o plano; quer o plano B. A aula do PRIMEIRO CONDICIONAL -- 'If the launch is delayed, we WILL use the backup plan' -- para apresentar os próximos lançamentos e, ao lado, o Rain Plan: o que você fará SE algo mudar. A linguagem de quem planeja com honestidade -- 35 slides

Extra Activities

Extra material to practice outside class. Check the box when you finish it.

Lesson 1 — Diagnostic + First Words -- Who Is Emmanuele Orrico?

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

In this first episode, almost every character has to say who they are and what they take care of — the same task as your lesson 1, at the office. It is business English at native speed, but the scenes are easy to follow: you understand the story even when you do not catch every word.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 moments when someone introduces themselves or describes their job. Do not try to understand 100% — the goal is to hear the structure "I'm... / I work... / I'm responsible for...".

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 400: Socializing with Colleagues 1 (Meeting New People)

You already listen to podcasts every day — now with a target. This episode is your lesson 1 in audio: people meeting at work, saying their name, their company, their job and what they take care of. The pace is made for A2-B1, with clear explanations and a lot of repetition.

Tip: listen ONCE on the way to work, with no pause — just let the sound in. Then listen again and repeat the sentences out loud. Your bottleneck is speaking, not listening: saying it out loud is worth more than understanding every word.

Listen ↗
YouTube
BBC Learning English — Introduce yourself and make some friends (English at Work, ep. 4)

The BBC made this series for people exactly like you: adults who work in a company and have to introduce themselves in English. This episode practices "My name is... / I work at... / I'm responsible for..." — the three sentences you take from lesson 1.

Tip: after the video, say 5 sentences about YOUR job at Sanofi out loud, with no notes — one for each disease area you manage. Record them on your phone and listen. Speaking alone, out loud, is the practice that opens your mouth in the meeting.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 2 — Your Day and Your Work Routine -- A Week at Sanofi Brazil

Series
Emily in Paris — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

A marketing executive arrives at a French company and spends the whole episode describing her routine — meetings, presentations, schedule, boss. It is your lesson 2 on screen, and the head office is in Paris, just like Sanofi Global.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 sentences where someone says what they do EVERY week (look for always, usually, every day, every morning). Do not try to understand 100% — the goal is to hear the structure of a routine.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 383: Routine Check-in Meeting

A routine call with the manager: what is in progress, what happened during the week, where the problems are. It is exactly the Monday call from lesson 2, with the ready-made sentences you need when the head office asks about your week.

Tip: listen ONE time on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat out loud the update sentences ("This week I am...", "Every Monday we..."). The problem is speaking, not listening.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Finding Pharma — Pharma Sales Representative: Day in the Life

An American pharma rep talks about his own day: schedule, visits, calls, reports. It is the vocabulary of lesson 2 (schedule, field visit, medical rep, reports) said by a person who lives it — and who is on the other side of your field team.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Write down 3 words you understood and 2 words you did NOT understand, and bring them to class. Then describe YOUR day out loud, using always / usually / often / sometimes / never.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 3 — What Happened Last Quarter? -- Reporting Past Results

Series
Painkiller — Episode 1 (Netflix)

A short series about the American pharmaceutical industry. Do not watch it for the story — watch it for the results meetings: targets, quarter numbers, the field team reporting back. It is the vocabulary of your Lesson 3 (target, results, quarter, growth) at the real speed of people who do this every day.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Write down 3 past sentences where someone talks about a number that ALREADY HAPPENED (look for -ed, "last quarter", "we hit / we missed"). Do not try to understand 100%.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 266: Presenting Numbers (1)

How to present numbers in English: say the target, say the result, compare it with the quarter before. It is exactly your August presentation, with the ready-made phrases the Global team expects to hear.

Tip: listen ONE time on the way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and say the result phrases out loud ("We achieved...", "We exceeded...", "We missed..."). Your speaking is the bottleneck, not your listening.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Simple Past Tense Examples for Business English

The simple past at work: ready-made meeting sentences, with the regular verbs (-ed) and the irregular verbs you saw in the lesson (went, had, gave, met, came).

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each example and say the SAME sentence with your own content (dermatology, asthma, market access). Write down 2 new irregular verbs and bring them to class.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 4 — Breaking the Ice -- Small Talk at a Global Event

Series
The Office (US) — Season 2, Episode 5: "Halloween" (Netflix)

Do not watch it for the comedy — watch it for the small talk. The whole office is made of hallway conversations: people introducing themselves, asking what the other person is doing, reacting. It is the exact mechanic of your lesson 4, in natural American English and in short sentences.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences that start with "I am working on..." or "We are..." (the structure of the NOW). Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the structure.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 261: Socializing and Networking (Part 1)

A full lesson on how to open a conversation at a corporate event: introduce yourself, ask what the other person does, and keep the conversation alive. It is literally the August dinner, with the ready-made sentences the Global team expects to hear.

Tip: listen ONE time on your way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and repeat out loud the opening sentences ("I do not think we have met...", "What brings you here?"). You already listen to a podcast every day — trade one for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Present Continuous vs Present Simple — English Grammar Lesson

The grammar of lesson 4, explained with examples: what you do ALWAYS (I work) against what you are doing NOW (I am working). It is the difference that decides if the executive understands that you have done that for ten years or only this month.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause at each example and say the SAME sentence with your content (immunology, asthma, the launch, the August presentation). Make 5 pairs: one "I work..." and one "I am working..." for each.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 5 — What Are Your Plans? -- Launches, Meetings and Goals

Series
The Diplomat — Season 1, Episode 1: "The Cinderella Thing" (Netflix)

Kate Wyler spends the whole series in meeting rooms, deciding what the country is going to do — and saying it out loud, under pressure, to people more powerful than her. It is your call with the Global team, with a different topic: the goal, the strategy, the deadline, and what is already in the calendar.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences that start with "We are going to..." or "I am going to...". Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the plan block.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 395: Strategic Decision-Making (1)

A whole meeting about goal and strategy: the team discusses what to prioritize, and why. It is the vocabulary of your lesson 5 in the mouth of executives, exactly the way Marc speaks.

Tip: listen ONCE on your way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and repeat out loud every sentence with "we are going to". You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Simple Future with Be Going To — Grammar & Verb Tenses (Ellii)

The grammar of lesson 5 explained slowly, with examples: the block am / is / are going to + the verb in its normal form, the negative and the question. It is the structure you are going to use in every sentence of the August plan.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each example and say the SAME sentence with your own content (the approval in May, the business plan, the August visit). Make 5 sentences about YOUR real calendar.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 6 — How Is It Going? -- Reporting Progress to the Global Team

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot" (Netflix)

Do not watch it for the story — watch it for the check-in. The whole office runs on the boss who stops at the door and asks your question of today: "where are we on this?", "how is it going?". And the answer always comes in the same shape: a number, a step forward, a problem.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 moments when someone asks for or gives a status. Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the shape of the answer.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 39c: Project Management English — Reporting on Progress

A full lesson on exactly what you practiced today: giving the status of a project in English. How to open the update, how to say what is on track, how to report a delay without saying sorry, and how to talk about quantity (how many / how much / a few / not much). It is the Friday call with Marc, with the sentences ready for you.

Tip: listen ONCE on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat the status sentences out loud. You already listen to podcasts every day — trade one of them for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
All American English — QUANTIFIERS: SOME or ANY? MUCH or MANY?

The grammar of lesson 6 explained in American English: when to use much and when to use many, and the difference between some and any. It is the one question that decides everything — can you count it? — explained with examples.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause at every example and say the SAME sentence with your own data: how many centers, how many doctors, how much progress, how much information. Make 5 pairs: one with how many and one with how much.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 7 — What's Next? -- Next Steps and Offers in the Global Meeting

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot" (Netflix)

Harvey Specter is the master of the offer on the spot. Every time someone brings him a problem, he answers in the moment: "I'll handle it," "I'll take care of it," "Consider it done." That is exactly the "I'll" of this lesson — the yes you give without freezing, in the middle of a meeting.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences that start with "I'll..." Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the offer on the spot.

Watch ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 81c: Action Points and Wrapping Up

A whole lesson about the end of a meeting: how to summarize the next steps, assign the actions, and close with a clear outcome. It is the last five minutes of your Global call, in the mouth of real executives — exactly the moment this lesson trains.

Tip: listen ONCE on your way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and repeat out loud every offer with "I'll" and every next step with "going to". You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Simple Future with Will — Grammar & Verb Tenses (Ellii)

The grammar of this lesson explained slowly, with examples: will and the contraction 'll for decisions, offers and promises, plus the negative won't and the question. It is the block you use for every "I'll send it" and "I'll get back to you".

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each example and make the SAME sentence with your own work (I'll send the report, I'll confirm the dates, I'll follow up). Make 5 offers about YOUR real meetings.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 8 — Better, Bigger, Best -- Comparing Results in the Business Review

Film
Moneyball (2011) — Official Trailer

A true story about a manager who stops looking at the biggest name and starts looking at the best figures. The whole film is one long comparison: which player is better, which is cheaper, where the real opportunity is. It is your lesson in a movie — bigger is not always better.

Tip: watch the trailer with English subtitles, then find the full film on your streaming service. Treasure hunt: write down 3 moments when someone compares two things — better, bigger, cheaper, the best.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 407: Comparing Opportunities

A full lesson on exactly what you practiced today: comparing two options in a business meeting. How to say one is bigger, higher or better than the other, how to name the best opportunity, and how to give your opinion. It is the business review with Marc, with the sentences ready for you.

Tip: listen ONCE on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat the comparisons out loud. You already listen to podcasts every day — trade one of them for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Business English Pod — 925 English Lesson 18: Using Superlatives

The grammar of lesson 8 in business English: how to name the number one with the biggest, the highest, the most important and the best. Short words add -est, long words take the most, and two words are irregular — explained with real work examples.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause at every example and say the SAME sentence with your own data: the biggest market, the highest growth, the most important priority, the best performance. Make 5 superlatives about your region.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 9 — Networking in English -- Starting and Keeping a Conversation

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot" (Netflix)

Harvey Specter walks into any room and opens a conversation with anyone — calm, confident, and always with a question. Watch how he breaks the ice and keeps a conversation alive. It is the social confidence you are building for the Global dinner, in a different world.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 questions a character uses to open or keep a conversation going. Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the conversation openers.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 155: English for Networking (Part 1)

Two people meet for the first time at a pharmacy conference — exactly your world. The lesson shows how to approach someone, introduce yourself, give a short "elevator pitch" about your job, and talk about your industry. This is the flow of your lesson in the mouth of real professionals.

Tip: listen ONCE on your way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and repeat out loud every opener you hear. You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
How to Start a Conversation at a Conference | Business English (Derek Callan)

Ready-made phrases to open, keep and close a conversation at a professional event — the exact five steps of your lesson, explained slowly and clearly. Perfect to review before the Global dinner in August.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each phrase and say it out loud with your own content (your name, Sanofi, immunology, the August plan). Choose your 3 favorite openers and learn them by heart.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 10 — How Much and How Many? -- Talking About Patients, Data and Market Access

Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 103c: Presentation Charts and Trends 1 (Describing Visuals)

Exactly the meeting you are training for: someone puts a chart of numbers on the screen, and you have to talk about it. This lesson gives you the sentences to describe the data, the trend and the amounts — how much went up, how many programs there are, what the figure shows — without freezing.

Tip: listen ONCE on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat the data sentences out loud with your own numbers — how many patients, how much evidence. You already listen to podcasts every day: trade one for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
All American English — QUANTIFIERS: SOME or ANY? MUCH or MANY?

The grammar of this lesson in American English: when to use much and when to use many, and the difference between some and any. It all comes back to one question — can you count it? — explained with clear examples.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause at each example and say the SAME sentence with your own data: how many patients, how many programs, how much evidence, how much funding. Make 5 pairs: one with how many and one with how much.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 5: "Bail Out" (Netflix)

Watch it for one thing: how professionals talk about numbers under pressure. The room is full of data — how many clients, how much money, how much time is left — and the person who wins is the one who says the number clearly, without apologizing. That is your business review.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 moments when someone says a quantity (a lot of, a few, not much). Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for how they say the amount.

Watch ↗

Lesson 11 — What's Happening Right Now -- Describing Live Situations

Series
Formula 1: Drive to Survive — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

On the pit wall, the teams describe what is happening on the track in real time: "The car is losing pace", "He is pushing now", "We are changing the strategy". It is the present continuous of a live situation — the same skill you use to report your field to the Global team.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences an engineer says about what is happening right now (am/is/are + -ing). Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the live descriptions.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 39: Reporting in English, Project Update

A project manager gives his boss a quick update on the status of a project — exactly your live update to David. You hear the natural phrases to open a status, say what is happening now, and report progress in a professional voice.

Tip: listen ONCE on your way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and repeat out loud every sentence that starts with "Right now, we are..." or "At the moment, we are...". You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
How to Give a Project Update in English at Work (Speak Confident English)

Ready-made phrases to give a clear status update at work — open the update, say what is happening now, report a challenge, and show you are in control. It is your lesson in a five-minute video, explained slowly and clearly.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each phrase and say it out loud with your own content (Sanofi, the launch, the South, in real time). Choose your 3 favorite update phrases and learn them by heart before the meeting with Global.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 12 — Plans in Motion -- Talking About What's Coming Next

Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 307: English for Project Management 2 (Kickoff Meeting)

Exactly the meeting behind this lesson: a team runs the kickoff of a new project and presents the plan for the coming months. You will hear the language of a plan already decided — the kickoff, the phases, the timeline, the milestones — the same words you are going to use with Paris.

Tip: listen ONCE on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat every "going to" sentence out loud with your own plan — your kickoff, your launch, your rollout. You already listen to podcasts every day: trade one for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Using "Going To" for Future Plans — Easy English Grammar Lesson

The grammar of this lesson, step by step: how to use going to for a plan you have already decided, with clear examples. It shows the pattern you practiced — am / is / are + going to + base verb — and the base verb that never changes.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause at each example and say the SAME sentence with your own plan: what you are going to launch, when the rollout is going to happen, where you are going to expand. Make 5 sentences about your real quarter.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 6: "Tricks of the Trade" (Netflix)

Watch it for one thing: how professionals present a plan for what they are going to do next. The room is full of strategy — the next steps, the plan, what they are going to do and when — and the person who wins is the one who says the plan clearly, with confidence. That is your presentation to Paris.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 moments when someone says what they are going to do (a plan, a next step). Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the future plans.

Watch ↗

Lesson 13 — Bigger, Better, Stronger -- Comparing Products, Markets and Results

Series
Formula 1: Drive to Survive — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

The teams compare everything: whose car is faster, who has the stronger engine, which team has the best strategy. Every race is a comparison — exactly the language you use to compare markets and results for the Global team.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 comparisons you hear (bigger, faster, stronger, the best, more competitive). Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for the comparatives and superlatives.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 103e: Describing Charts and Trends (1)

A manager presents market-share data and compares his company to the competition — higher, lower, stronger, the biggest. It is your comparison to David and the VP, in a natural professional voice.

Tip: listen ONCE on your way to work, with no pauses. Then listen again and repeat out loud every sentence that compares two numbers ("higher than...", "the biggest..."). You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
How to Use Comparatives and Superlatives — 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

Six clear minutes on the exact grammar of this lesson: when to add -er and -est, when to use more and most, and the irregulars good/better/best. Slow, clear British teachers — perfect to review before your next Global review.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each rule and say one example with your own content (Brazil, growth, atopic dermatitis, efficacy). Learn the irregulars by heart: good → better → best, bad → worse → worst.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 14 — Checking In and Speaking Up -- Asking for Clarification in Global Meetings

Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 35c: Clarifying What Was Meant

A full lesson on exactly what you practiced today: how to clarify what someone means in a meeting, so a small misunderstanding never becomes a big one. It gives you the ready phrases — "What do you mean by...?", "Just to clarify..." — with real dialogue you can copy word for word.

Tip: listen ONCE on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat the clarifying phrases out loud. You already listen to podcasts every day — trade one of them for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
Derek Callan — How To Interrupt Someone Politely (Business English Meetings)

The other half of today's toolkit: how to interrupt and hold the floor without sounding rude. Short, clear phrases for the exact moment when you want to speak but the meeting is moving fast — "Sorry to interrupt, but...", "Can I just add one thing?".

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause at every phrase and say it out loud twice. Then choose your two favorites and keep them ready for your next Global call.

Watch on YouTube ↗
Film
The Intern (2015) — Official Trailer

A warm story about a senior professional who joins a fast, modern company — full of meetings, quick questions and people speaking over each other. Watch how the calm person in the room is the one who asks, listens, and raises a point at the right moment. It is your lesson in a movie: you don't have to be the loudest, only the clearest.

Tip: watch the trailer with English subtitles, then find the full film on your streaming service. Treasure hunt: write down 3 moments when someone asks a question to check they understood.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 15 — Have You Replied Yet? -- Handling the Global Inbox on a Follow-up Call

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

A busy law firm runs on emails, follow-ups and deadlines: "Have you sent it?", "I've already replied", "I haven't finished it yet." It is exactly the fast, professional English you use on a call with the Global team.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences with the present perfect (have sent, have just called, haven't done it yet). Do not try to understand 100% — look ONLY for have / has + verb.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast
Speak Confident English — Write Professional Emails in English

A clear lesson on the exact language of your inbox: how to reply, follow up, forward a thread and say what is still open. Natural, professional phrases you can reuse on your next follow-up call with David.

Tip: listen once with no pauses, then again and repeat out loud every sentence with the present perfect ("I've attached...", "I've already sent..."). You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
The present perfect with 'just', 'already' and 'yet' — 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

Six clear minutes on the exact grammar of this lesson: how to build the present perfect and when to use just, already and yet. Slow, clear British teachers — perfect to review before your next Global follow-up call.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each rule and say one example with your own content (report, thread, reminder, access summary). Say them out loud: "I've just sent it", "I've already replied", "I haven't finished it yet".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 16 — What Comes Next? -- Forecasting Brazil's Results for the Global Team

Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 267: Presenting Numbers

Exactly what you did today: how to present numbers and turn them into a story about the future. It shows you how to highlight the important figures, draw a conclusion, and predict what will happen next — the language of the forecast, with a real dialogue you can copy.

Tip: listen once on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat every "will" sentence out loud. You already listen to podcasts every day — trade one of them for your English.

Listen ↗
YouTube
engVid — Future: "will" or "going to"?

The exact grammar of today's class, in one clear lesson. Teacher Adam shows the difference between will (what you predict will happen) and going to (a plan you already decided) — the one choice that makes your forecast sound right in a Global meeting.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each example and say your own version out loud, about the Brazil numbers: "Sales will increase", "We are going to launch in Q3".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Film
Moneyball (2011) — Official Trailer

A true story about a manager who reads the future in the numbers. Instead of old opinions, Billy Beane uses data to predict which players will perform — a whole team built on a projection. It is your lesson in a movie: the person who trusts the forecast, not the noise, is the one who wins.

Tip: watch the trailer with English subtitles, then find the full film on your streaming service. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences where someone predicts what will happen.

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 17 — Midpoint Milestone -- Progress Review: Looking Back at 16 Weeks

Series
Suits — Season 1, Episode 1 (Netflix)

A busy firm where people present results, review their progress and talk about targets and challenges — the same professional English you use with the Global team. A good mirror for your midpoint review.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Treasure hunt: write down 3 sentences that compare or review — one with a comparative ("stronger than", "better than") and two about results or a challenge. Do not try to understand 100%.

Watch on Netflix ↗
Podcast & Lesson
Speak Confident English — Talk About Your Experience and Progress

A clear lesson to talk about what you have done and how far you have come — exactly what you did in your midpoint review. Natural, professional phrases you can reuse when you describe your progress to the Global team.

Tip: listen or read once, then say out loud one sentence about your own progress: "Looking back at the first half, I..." You already listen to podcasts every day — give one of them to your English.

Read & listen ↗
YouTube
Comparatives and superlatives — 6 Minute Grammar (BBC Learning English)

Six clear minutes to review one of the five structures of this lesson: how to compare things with "-er than" (stronger than, better than) and superlatives. Slow, clear British teachers — perfect before your next Global review call.

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each rule and say one comparison from your own work: "This year is stronger than last year", "Our growth is better than last quarter".

Watch on YouTube ↗

Lesson 18 — The Rain Plan -- Presenting Upcoming Launches and the Backup Plan to Global

YouTube
engVid — Conditionals: Zero & First Conditionals

The exact grammar of today's class, with teacher Ronnie. She shows the first conditional step by step: if + the present, then will + the simple verb. This is the pattern of every backup plan you built today — "If the launch is delayed, we will postpone it."

Tip: turn on the English subtitles. Pause after each example and say your own version out loud, about the Brazil plan: "If it rains, we will move online", "If we are behind schedule, we will reschedule the congress".

Watch on YouTube ↗
Podcast
Business English Pod — BEP 391: Scoping a Project (1)

Exactly the situation of your class: how to present a plan for something in the future — the scope, the timeline, and what happens if things change. It gives you the real phrases to talk about a launch, a deadline and a backup plan, in a natural business dialogue you can copy.

Tip: listen once on the way to work, with no pause. Then listen again and repeat every "if... will..." sentence out loud. You already listen to podcasts every day — trade one of them for your English.

Listen ↗
Film
Apollo 13 (1995) — Official Trailer

The greatest Rain Plan story ever told. When the normal plan fails in space, the team on the ground builds one backup plan after another to bring the crew home: "If we do this, they will have air; if we do that, they will have power." It is your lesson as a true story — the people who are ready for every setback are the ones who win.

Tip: watch the trailer with English subtitles, then find the full film on your streaming service. Treasure hunt: write down 3 moments where someone says what they will do if something goes wrong.

Watch on YouTube ↗

T
